**225.** `[C0274 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: For examples of the use of subsidiary and incidental motions in the handling of main motions and the _____ of privileged motions while a main motion is pending, see 11–33.)

   *Answer:* application — “For examples of the use of subsidiary and incidental motions in the handling of main motions and the application of privileged motions while a main motion is pending, see 11–33.)”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:43 — “For examples of the use of subsidiary and incidental motions in the handling of main motions and the application of privileged motions while a main motion is pending, see 11–33.)”

**226.** `[C0275 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The term _____ (or notice), as applied to necessary conditions for the adoption of certain motions, has a particular meaning in parliamentary law.

   *Answer:* previous notice — “The term previous notice (or notice), as applied to necessary conditions for the adoption of certain motions, has a particular meaning in parliamentary law.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:44 — “The term previous notice (or notice), as applied to necessary conditions for the adoption of certain motions, has a particular meaning in parliamentary law.”

**227.** `[C0275 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The term previous _____ (or notice), as applied to necessary conditions for the adoption of certain motions, has a particular meaning in parliamentary law.
   A. notice
   B. Abuses
   C. Speech
   D. accept

   *Answer:* A) notice. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:44 — “The term previous notice (or notice), as applied to necessary conditions for the adoption of certain motions, has a particular meaning in parliamentary law.”

**228.** `[C0276 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Motions that have the effect of changing or nullifying previous action of the assembly—such as the motion to _____ or to Amend Something Previously Adopted (35), the motion to Discharge a Committee (36), or a motion to postpone an event already scheduled—require previous notice if they are to be adopted by only a majority vote.

   *Answer:* Rescind — “Motions that have the effect of changing or nullifying previous action of the assembly—such as the motion to Rescind or to Amend Something Previously Adopted (35), the motion to Discharge a Committee (36), or a motion to postpone an event already scheduled—require previous notice if they are to be adopted by only a majority vote.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:45 — “Motions that have the effect of changing or nullifying previous action of the assembly—such as the motion to Rescind or to Amend Something Previously Adopted (35), the motion to Discharge a Committee (36), or a motion to postpone an event already scheduled—require previous notice if they are to be adopted by only a majority vote.”

**229.** `[C0276 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Motions that have the effect of changing or nullifying previous action of the assembly—such as the motion to Rescind or to _____ (35), the motion to Discharge a Committee (36), or a motion to postpone an event already scheduled—require previous notice if they are to be adopted by only a majority vote.
   A. Appointment of the parliamentarian
   B. Amend Something Previously Adopted
   C. Question Unless Separately Ordered
   D. Question; Interruption of Execution

   *Answer:* B) Amend Something Previously Adopted. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:45 — “Motions that have the effect of changing or nullifying previous action of the assembly—such as the motion to Rescind or to Amend Something Previously Adopted (35), the motion to Discharge a Committee (36), or a motion to postpone an event already scheduled—require previous notice if they are to be adopted by only a majority vote.”

**230.** `[C0276 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The adoption or amendment of special rules of order requires either (a) previous notice _____ a two-thirds vote or (b) a vote of a majority of the entire membership—as does the amendment of bylaws if they do not prescribe the procedure for their amendment, which they should do (see also Table of Rules Relating to Motions, pages t6–t33).
   A. event
   B. Forum
   C. and
   D. clerk

   *Answer:* C) and. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:45 — “The adoption or amendment of special rules of order requires either (a) previous notice and a two-thirds vote or (b) a vote of a majority of the entire membership—as does the amendment of bylaws if they do not prescribe the procedure for their amendment, which they should do (see also Table of Rules Relating to Motions, pages t6–t33).”

**231.** `[C0276 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The adoption or amendment of special rules of order requires either (a) previous notice _____ a two-thirds vote or (b) a vote of a majority of the entire membership—as does the amendment of bylaws if they do not prescribe the procedure for their amendment, which they should do (see also Table of Rules Relating to Motions, pages t6–t33).

   *Answer:* and — “The adoption or amendment of special rules of order requires either (a) previous notice and a two-thirds vote or (b) a vote of a majority of the entire membership—as does the amendment of bylaws if they do not prescribe the procedure for their amendment, which they should do (see also Table of Rules Relating to Motions, pages t6–t33).”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:45 — “The adoption or amendment of special rules of order requires either (a) previous notice and a two-thirds vote or (b) a vote of a majority of the entire membership—as does the amendment of bylaws if they do not prescribe the procedure for their amendment, which they should do (see also Table of Rules Relating to Motions, pages t6–t33).”

**232.** `[C0277 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Subject to any rules of the _____ that provide how notice shall be given, it can be given as follows:

   *Answer:* organization — “Subject to any rules of the organization that provide how notice shall be given, it can be given as follows:”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:46 — “Subject to any rules of the organization that provide how notice shall be given, it can be given as follows:”

**233.** `[C0277 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Subject to any rules of the _____ that provide how notice shall be given, it can be given as follows:
   A. Preparation
   B. adjournment
   C. Definitely
   D. organization

   *Answer:* D) organization. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:46 — “Subject to any rules of the organization that provide how notice shall be given, it can be given as follows:”

**234.** `[C0278 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: If previous notice is given _____, it can be given orally unless the rules of the organization require it to be in writing—which is often the case with notice of amendments to bylaws.

   *Answer:* at a meeting — “If previous notice is given at a meeting, it can be given orally unless the rules of the organization require it to be in writing—which is often the case with notice of amendments to bylaws.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:47 — “If previous notice is given at a meeting, it can be given orally unless the rules of the organization require it to be in writing—which is often the case with notice of amendments to bylaws.”

**235.** `[C0278 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: If previous notice is given at a meeting, it can be given orally unless the rules of the _____ require it to be in writing—which is often the case with notice of amendments to bylaws.
   A. Definitely
   B. constitution
   C. organization
   D. Announcements

   *Answer:* C) organization. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:47 — “If previous notice is given at a meeting, it can be given orally unless the rules of the organization require it to be in writing—which is often the case with notice of amendments to bylaws.”

**236.** `[C0278 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Unless the rules require the full text of the motion, _____, or bylaw amendment to be submitted in the notice, only the purport need be indicated;

   *Answer:* resolution — “Unless the rules require the full text of the motion, resolution, or bylaw amendment to be submitted in the notice, only the purport need be indicated;”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:47 — “Unless the rules require the full text of the motion, resolution, or bylaw amendment to be submitted in the notice, only the purport need be indicated;”

**237.** `[C0278 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The notice _____ invalid if the motion is amended beyond the scope of the notice (see also 35, 57).

   *Answer:* becomes — “The notice becomes invalid if the motion is amended beyond the scope of the notice (see also 35, 57).”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:47 — “The notice becomes invalid if the motion is amended beyond the scope of the notice (see also 35, 57).”

**238.** `[C0278 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Unless the rules require the full text of the motion, _____, or bylaw amendment to be submitted in the notice, only the purport need be indicated;
   A. Substitute
   B. committees
   C. resolution
   D. settlement

   *Answer:* C) resolution. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:47 — “Unless the rules require the full text of the motion, resolution, or bylaw amendment to be submitted in the notice, only the purport need be indicated;”

**239.** `[C0279 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: But if the member wishing to give the notice is unable to obtain the floor while no business is pending (as may sometimes happen, for example, in a _____ that is following an adopted agenda or program, 41, or in cases where a meeting of an ordinary society adjourns before completing its regular order of business), the notice, if necessary, can interrupt pending business or any other pending motion;

   *Answer:* convention — “But if the member wishing to give the notice is unable to obtain the floor while no business is pending (as may sometimes happen, for example, in a convention that is following an adopted agenda or program, 41, or in cases where a meeting of an ordinary society adjourns before completing its regular order of business), the notice, if necessary, can interrupt pending business or any other pending motion;”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:48 — “But if the member wishing to give the notice is unable to obtain the floor while no business is pending (as may sometimes happen, for example, in a convention that is following an adopted agenda or program, 41, or in cases where a meeting of an ordinary society adjourns before completing its regular order of business), the notice, if necessary, can interrupt pending business or any other pending motion;”

**240.** `[C0279 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: But if the member wishing to give the notice is unable to obtain the floor while no business is pending (as may sometimes happen, for example, in a _____ that is following an adopted agenda or program, 41, or in cases where a meeting of an ordinary society adjourns before completing its regular order of business), the notice, if necessary, can interrupt pending business or any other pending motion;
   A. resolution
   B. committees
   C. settlement
   D. convention

   *Answer:* D) convention. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:48 — “But if the member wishing to give the notice is unable to obtain the floor while no business is pending (as may sometimes happen, for example, in a convention that is following an adopted agenda or program, 41, or in cases where a meeting of an ordinary society adjourns before completing its regular order of business), the notice, if necessary, can interrupt pending business or any other pending motion;”

**241.** `[C0280 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The _____ then records the notice in the minutes.

   *Answer:* secretary — “The secretary then records the notice in the minutes.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:50 — “The secretary then records the notice in the minutes.”

**242.** `[C0280 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: If the member desiring to give the notice is unable to obtain the floor, the following variations in form can be used as _____ to the case:
   A. Incidental
   B. Preparation
   C. adjournment
   D. appropriate

   *Answer:* D) appropriate. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:50 — “If the member desiring to give the notice is unable to obtain the floor, the following variations in form can be used as appropriate to the case:”

**243.** `[C0280 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The _____ then records the notice in the minutes.
   A. Precedent
   B. unamended
   C. paragraph
   D. secretary

   *Answer:* D) secretary. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:50 — “The secretary then records the notice in the minutes.”

**244.** `[C0280 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: If the member desiring to give the notice is unable to obtain the floor, the following variations in form can be used as _____ to the case:

   *Answer:* appropriate — “If the member desiring to give the notice is unable to obtain the floor, the following variations in form can be used as appropriate to the case:”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:50 — “If the member desiring to give the notice is unable to obtain the floor, the following variations in form can be used as appropriate to the case:”

**245.** `[C0281 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Instead of being given at a meeting, a notice can also be sent to every member with the call of the meeting at which the matter is to come up for action, in cases where there is a duty or _____ custom of issuing such a call.

   *Answer:* established — “Instead of being given at a meeting, a notice can also be sent to every member with the call of the meeting at which the matter is to come up for action, in cases where there is a duty or established custom of issuing such a call.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:51 — “Instead of being given at a meeting, a notice can also be sent to every member with the call of the meeting at which the matter is to come up for action, in cases where there is a duty or established custom of issuing such a call.”

**246.** `[C0281 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: In such cases, the member desiring to give the notice writes to the secretary alone, _____ that the notice be sent with the call of the next meeting, and the secretary then does this at the expense of the society.
   A. substitute
   B. requesting
   C. Permission
   D. persuasive

   *Answer:* B) requesting. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:51 — “In such cases, the member desiring to give the notice writes to the secretary alone, requesting that the notice be sent with the call of the next meeting, and the secretary then does this at the expense of the society.”

**247.** `[C0281 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Instead of being given at a meeting, a notice can also be sent to every member with the call of the meeting at which the matter is to come up for action, in cases where there is a duty or _____ custom of issuing such a call.
   A. immediately
   B. Definitely
   C. established
   D. Incidental

   *Answer:* C) established. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:51 — “Instead of being given at a meeting, a notice can also be sent to every member with the call of the meeting at which the matter is to come up for action, in cases where there is a duty or established custom of issuing such a call.”

**248.** `[C0281 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: In such cases, the member desiring to give the notice writes to the secretary alone, _____ that the notice be sent with the call of the next meeting, and the secretary then does this at the expense of the society.

   *Answer:* requesting — “In such cases, the member desiring to give the notice writes to the secretary alone, requesting that the notice be sent with the call of the next meeting, and the secretary then does this at the expense of the society.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:51 — “In such cases, the member desiring to give the notice writes to the secretary alone, requesting that the notice be sent with the call of the next meeting, and the secretary then does this at the expense of the society.”

**249.** `[C0282 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: A motion to _____ (or accept or agree to) an officer’s or a committee’s report or recommendations which the assembly (by means of a main motion) directed the officer or committee to prepare is an incidental main motion.

   *Answer:* adopt — “A motion to adopt (or accept or agree to) an officer’s or a committee’s report or recommendations which the assembly (by means of a main motion) directed the officer or committee to prepare is an incidental main motion.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:52 — “A motion to adopt (or accept or agree to) an officer’s or a committee’s report or recommendations which the assembly (by means of a main motion) directed the officer or committee to prepare is an incidental main motion.”

**250.** `[C0282 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: A motion to adopt (or _____ or agree to) an officer’s or a committee’s report or recommendations which the assembly (by means of a main motion) directed the officer or committee to prepare is an incidental main motion.
   A. accept
   B. adhere
   C. Motion
   D. common

   *Answer:* A) accept. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:52 — “A motion to adopt (or accept or agree to) an officer’s or a committee’s report or recommendations which the assembly (by means of a main motion) directed the officer or committee to prepare is an incidental main motion.”

**251.** `[C0283 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: A motion to adopt a _____, bylaws, or any other document can be amended by adding, “and that it be printed and that members be furnished with copies,” or, “that it [or “they”] go into effect at the close of this annual meeting,” or anything of a similar nature (see also 51;

   *Answer:* resolution — “A motion to adopt a resolution, bylaws, or any other document can be amended by adding, “and that it be printed and that members be furnished with copies,” or, “that it [or “they”] go into effect at the close of this annual meeting,” or anything of a similar nature (see also 51;”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:53 — “A motion to adopt a resolution, bylaws, or any other document can be amended by adding, “and that it be printed and that members be furnished with copies,” or, “that it [or “they”] go into effect at the close of this annual meeting,” or anything of a similar nature (see also 51;”

**252.** `[C0283 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: A motion to adopt a _____, bylaws, or any other document can be amended by adding, “and that it be printed and that members be furnished with copies,” or, “that it [or “they”] go into effect at the close of this annual meeting,” or anything of a similar nature (see also 51;
   A. Substitute
   B. resolution
   C. landscaped
   D. sustaining

   *Answer:* B) resolution. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:53 — “A motion to adopt a resolution, bylaws, or any other document can be amended by adding, “and that it be printed and that members be furnished with copies,” or, “that it [or “they”] go into effect at the close of this annual meeting,” or anything of a similar nature (see also 51;”

**253.** `[C0284 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The motion to _____ (also called approve or confirm) is an incidental main motion that is used to confirm or make valid an action already taken that cannot become valid until approved by the assembly.

   *Answer:* ratify — “The motion to ratify (also called approve or confirm) is an incidental main motion that is used to confirm or make valid an action already taken that cannot become valid until approved by the assembly.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:54 — “The motion to ratify (also called approve or confirm) is an incidental main motion that is used to confirm or make valid an action already taken that cannot become valid until approved by the assembly.”

**254.** `[C0284 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The motion to ratify (also called _____ or confirm) is an incidental main motion that is used to confirm or make valid an action already taken that cannot become valid until approved by the assembly.
   A. approve
   B. adjourn
   C. Regular
   D. Divided

   *Answer:* A) approve. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:54 — “The motion to ratify (also called approve or confirm) is an incidental main motion that is used to confirm or make valid an action already taken that cannot become valid until approved by the assembly.”

**255.** `[C0284 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Cases where the procedure of _____ is applicable include:

   *Answer:* ratification — “Cases where the procedure of ratification is applicable include:”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:54 — “Cases where the procedure of ratification is applicable include:”

**256.** `[C0284 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Cases where the procedure of _____ is applicable include:
   A. Preparation
   B. deliberative
   C. ratification
   D. adjournment

   *Answer:* C) ratification. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:54 — “Cases where the procedure of ratification is applicable include:”

**257.** `[C0285 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: An assembly can ratify only such actions of its officers, committees, delegates, _____ bodies, or staff as it would have had the right to authorize in advance.

   *Answer:* subordinate — “An assembly can ratify only such actions of its officers, committees, delegates, subordinate bodies, or staff as it would have had the right to authorize in advance.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:55 — “An assembly can ratify only such actions of its officers, committees, delegates, subordinate bodies, or staff as it would have had the right to authorize in advance.”

**258.** `[C0285 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: It cannot make valid a _____ election when the bylaws require elections to be by ballot;
   A. Procedures
   B. assemblies
   C. voice-vote
   D. Substitute

   *Answer:* C) voice-vote. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:55 — “It cannot make valid a voice-vote election when the bylaws require elections to be by ballot;”

**259.** `[C0285 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: nor can it ratify anything done in violation of _____ rules prescribed by national, state, or local law, or in violation of its own bylaws, except that provision for a quorum in the bylaws does not prevent it from ratifying action taken at a meeting when no quorum was present.

   *Answer:* procedural — “nor can it ratify anything done in violation of procedural rules prescribed by national, state, or local law, or in violation of its own bylaws, except that provision for a quorum in the bylaws does not prevent it from ratifying action taken at a meeting when no quorum was present.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:55 — “nor can it ratify anything done in violation of procedural rules prescribed by national, state, or local law, or in violation of its own bylaws, except that provision for a quorum in the bylaws does not prevent it from ratifying action taken at a meeting when no quorum was present.”

**260.** `[C0285 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: An assembly can ratify only such actions of its officers, committees, delegates, _____ bodies, or staff as it would have had the right to authorize in advance.
   A. Information
   B. subordinate
   C. Definitely
   D. Reconsider

   *Answer:* B) subordinate. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:55 — “An assembly can ratify only such actions of its officers, committees, delegates, subordinate bodies, or staff as it would have had the right to authorize in advance.”

**261.** `[C0285 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: It cannot make valid a _____ election when the bylaws require elections to be by ballot;

   *Answer:* voice-vote — “It cannot make valid a voice-vote election when the bylaws require elections to be by ballot;”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:55 — “It cannot make valid a voice-vote election when the bylaws require elections to be by ballot;”

**262.** `[C0286 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: A motion to ratify can be amended by substituting a motion of censure, and vice versa, when the action involved has been taken by an officer or other _____ of the assembly.

   *Answer:* representative — “A motion to ratify can be amended by substituting a motion of censure, and vice versa, when the action involved has been taken by an officer or other representative of the assembly.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:56 — “A motion to ratify can be amended by substituting a motion of censure, and vice versa, when the action involved has been taken by an officer or other representative of the assembly.”

**263.** `[C0286 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: A motion to ratify can be amended by substituting a motion of censure, and vice versa, when the action involved has been taken by an officer or other _____ of the assembly.
   A. Responsibilities
   B. specification
   C. resolutions
   D. representative

   *Answer:* D) representative. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:56 — “A motion to ratify can be amended by substituting a motion of censure, and vice versa, when the action involved has been taken by an officer or other representative of the assembly.”

**264.** `[C0287 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Since the motion to ratify (or to censure) is a main motion, it is _____ and opens the entire question to debate.

   *Answer:* debatable — “Since the motion to ratify (or to censure) is a main motion, it is debatable and opens the entire question to debate.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:57 — “Since the motion to ratify (or to censure) is a main motion, it is debatable and opens the entire question to debate.”

**265.** `[C0287 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Since the motion to ratify (or to censure) is a main motion, it is _____ and opens the entire question to debate.
   A. Objection
   B. rejecting
   C. debatable
   D. extending

   *Answer:* C) debatable. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:57 — “Since the motion to ratify (or to censure) is a main motion, it is debatable and opens the entire question to debate.”

---

## Domain 2: Subsidiary and Privileged Motions

**1.** `[K0001 · Vote required]` What vote does the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn require to be adopted?
   A. Majority vote
   B. Two-thirds vote
   C. No vote (chair rules / demand)
   D. Unanimous consent

   *Answer:* Majority vote. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:2 — “A motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn is in order only if at the time it is offered there is no meeting scheduled for later within the same session.”

**2.** `[K0002 · Debatability]` Is the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn debatable?
   A. Debatable
   B. Not debatable

   *Answer:* Not debatable. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:15 — “Amendment as to time and place is possible, but no debate is in order.”

**3.** `[K0003 · Second required]` Does the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn require a second?
   A. Requires a second
   B. No second required

   *Answer:* Requires a second. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:17 — “As a second example, assume that the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn is to be made with a view to immediate adjournment to a specified time, when this purpose cannot be reached by a main motion:”

**4.** `[K0004 · Amendability]` Can the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn be amended?
   A. Amendable
   B. Not amendable

   *Answer:* Amendable. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:2 — “If there is such a meeting, additional meetings within the same session may be set by a motion either to Suspend the Rules (25) or to Amend Something Previously Adopted (35), namely, the previously adopted agenda or program for the session.”

**5.** `[K0005 · Vote required]` What vote does the motion to Adjourn require to be adopted?
   A. Majority vote
   B. Two-thirds vote
   C. No vote (chair rules / demand)
   D. Unanimous consent

   *Answer:* Majority vote. See RONR (12th ed.) §21:13 — “Since a motion to Adjourn may be voted down because a majority wish to hear one speech or take one vote, this motion must be renewable as soon as there has been any progress in business or even material progress in debate.”

**6.** `[K0006 · Debatability]` Is the motion to Adjourn debatable?
   A. Debatable
   B. Not debatable

   *Answer:* Not debatable. See RONR (12th ed.) §21:13 — “Since a motion to Adjourn may be voted down because a majority wish to hear one speech or take one vote, this motion must be renewable as soon as there has been any progress in business or even material progress in debate.”

**7.** `[K0007 · Second required]` Does the motion to Adjourn require a second?
   A. Requires a second
   B. No second required

   *Answer:* Requires a second. See RONR (12th ed.) §21:17 — “Assume that while a resolution is pending in a regular monthly meeting of a local society, a member obtains the floor and moves to adjourn, and the motion is seconded.”

**8.** `[K0008 · Amendability]` Can the motion to Adjourn be amended?
   A. Amendable
   B. Not amendable

   *Answer:* Not amendable. See RONR (12th ed.) §21:1 — “To adjourn means to close the meeting (8).”

**9.** `[K0009 · Vote required]` What vote does the motion to Recess require to be adopted?
   A. Majority vote
   B. Two-thirds vote
   C. No vote (chair rules / demand)
   D. Unanimous consent

   *Answer:* Majority vote. See RONR (12th ed.) §20:1 — “A recess is a short intermission in the assembly’s proceedings, commonly of only a few minutes, which does not close the meeting and after which business will immediately be resumed at exactly the point where it was interrupted.”

**10.** `[K0010 · Debatability]` Is the motion to Recess debatable?
   A. Debatable
   B. Not debatable

   *Answer:* Not debatable. See RONR (12th ed.) §20:1 — “A recess is a short intermission in the assembly’s proceedings, commonly of only a few minutes, which does not close the meeting and after which business will immediately be resumed at exactly the point where it was interrupted.”

**11.** `[K0011 · Second required]` Does the motion to Recess require a second?
   A. Requires a second
   B. No second required

   *Answer:* Requires a second. See RONR (12th ed.) §20:1 — “A recess is a short intermission in the assembly’s proceedings, commonly of only a few minutes, which does not close the meeting and after which business will immediately be resumed at exactly the point where it was interrupted.”

**12.** `[K0012 · Amendability]` Can the motion to Recess be amended?
   A. Amendable
   B. Not amendable

   *Answer:* Amendable. See RONR (12th ed.) §20:1 — “A recess is a short intermission in the assembly’s proceedings, commonly of only a few minutes, which does not close the meeting and after which business will immediately be resumed at exactly the point where it was interrupted.”

**13.** `[K0013 · Vote required]` What vote does the motion to Raise a Question of Privilege require to be adopted?
   A. Majority vote
   B. Two-thirds vote
   C. No vote (chair rules / demand)
   D. Unanimous consent

   *Answer:* No vote (chair rules / demand). See RONR (12th ed.) §19:2 — “the chair must then rule (subject to appeal, 24) whether the request or motion is in fact a question of privilege and, if so, whether it is urgent enough to interrupt the pending business.”

**14.** `[K0014 · Debatability]` Is the motion to Raise a Question of Privilege debatable?
   A. Debatable
   B. Not debatable

   *Answer:* Not debatable. See RONR (12th ed.) §19:2 — “the chair must then rule (subject to appeal, 24) whether the request or motion is in fact a question of privilege and, if so, whether it is urgent enough to interrupt the pending business.”

**15.** `[K0015 · Second required]` Does the motion to Raise a Question of Privilege require a second?
   A. Requires a second
   B. No second required

   *Answer:* No second required. See RONR (12th ed.) §19:3 — “Questions of privilege can also be introduced while no motion is pending, either as requests or by being moved and seconded just as any other main motion;”

**16.** `[K0016 · Amendability]` Can the motion to Raise a Question of Privilege be amended?
   A. Amendable
   B. Not amendable

   *Answer:* Not amendable. See RONR (12th ed.) §19:2 — “the chair must then rule (subject to appeal, 24) whether the request or motion is in fact a question of privilege and, if so, whether it is urgent enough to interrupt the pending business.”

**17.** `[K0017 · Vote required]` What vote does the motion to Call for the Orders of the Day require to be adopted?
   A. Majority vote
   B. Two-thirds vote
   C. No vote (chair rules / demand)
   D. Unanimous consent

   *Answer:* No vote (chair rules / demand). See RONR (12th ed.) §18:3 — “In other words, while the member may remind the chair of what is scheduled, he cannot by this call obtain consideration of an order of the day that does not have first priority for consideration at that time.”

**18.** `[K0018 · Debatability]` Is the motion to Call for the Orders of the Day debatable?
   A. Debatable
   B. Not debatable

   *Answer:* Not debatable. See RONR (12th ed.) §18:2 — “Taking up business in the prescribed order is of substantial importance, especially in conventions—which must follow a closely regulated schedule with much of the underlying work taking place off the convention floor in conferences and committees.”

**19.** `[K0019 · Second required]` Does the motion to Call for the Orders of the Day require a second?
   A. Requires a second
   B. No second required

   *Answer:* No second required. See RONR (12th ed.) §18:2 — “Taking up business in the prescribed order is of substantial importance, especially in conventions—which must follow a closely regulated schedule with much of the underlying work taking place off the convention floor in conferences and committees.”

**20.** `[K0020 · Amendability]` Can the motion to Call for the Orders of the Day be amended?
   A. Amendable
   B. Not amendable

   *Answer:* Not amendable. See RONR (12th ed.) §18:2 — “Taking up business in the prescribed order is of substantial importance, especially in conventions—which must follow a closely regulated schedule with much of the underlying work taking place off the convention floor in conferences and committees.”

**21.** `[K0021 · Vote required]` What vote does the motion to Lay on the Table require to be adopted?
   A. Majority vote
   B. Two-thirds vote
   C. No vote (chair rules / demand)
   D. Unanimous consent

   *Answer:* Majority vote. See RONR (12th ed.) §17:2 — “By adopting the motion to Lay on the Table, a majority has the power to halt consideration of a question immediately without debate.”

**22.** `[K0022 · Debatability]` Is the motion to Lay on the Table debatable?
   A. Debatable
   B. Not debatable

   *Answer:* Not debatable. See RONR (12th ed.) §17:2 — “By adopting the motion to Lay on the Table, a majority has the power to halt consideration of a question immediately without debate.”

**23.** `[K0023 · Second required]` Does the motion to Lay on the Table require a second?
   A. Requires a second
   B. No second required

   *Answer:* Requires a second. See RONR (12th ed.) §17:15 — “the second is in violation of the fundamental principle of parliamentary law that only a two-thirds vote can rightfully suppress a main question without allowing free debate.”

**24.** `[K0024 · Amendability]` Can the motion to Lay on the Table be amended?
   A. Amendable
   B. Not amendable

   *Answer:* Not amendable. See RONR (12th ed.) §17:5 — “Thus, if a resolution with a proposed amendment and a motion to Commit are pending and the resolution is laid on the table, all of these questions go to the table at the same time and, if taken from the table, all will return together.”

**25.** `[K0025 · Vote required]` What vote does the motion for the Previous Question require to be adopted?
   A. Majority vote
   B. Two-thirds vote
   C. No vote (chair rules / demand)
   D. Unanimous consent

   *Answer:* Two-thirds vote. See RONR (12th ed.) §16:16 — “consequently, if a majority have voted for reconsideration, it will be impossible to obtain a two-thirds vote in favor of the Previous Question.”

**26.** `[K0026 · Debatability]` Is the motion for the Previous Question debatable?
   A. Debatable
   B. Not debatable

   *Answer:* Not debatable. See RONR (12th ed.) §16:7 — “Sometimes the mere making of a motion for the Previous Question or “call for the question” may motivate unanimous consent to ending debate.”

**27.** `[K0027 · Second required]` Does the motion for the Previous Question require a second?
   A. Requires a second
   B. No second required

   *Answer:* Requires a second. See RONR (12th ed.) §16:7 — “or, if it has already been seconded, he must immediately take a vote on whether to order the Previous Question.”

**28.** `[K0028 · Amendability]` Can the motion for the Previous Question be amended?
   A. Amendable
   B. Not amendable

   *Answer:* Not amendable. See RONR (12th ed.) §16:8 — “After the chair has declared the preamble open to debate and amendment in such a case, the entire resolution can be brought to an immediate vote, if desired, by then ordering the Previous Question on the preamble.”

**29.** `[K0029 · Vote required]` What vote does the motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate require to be adopted?
   A. Majority vote
   B. Two-thirds vote
   C. No vote (chair rules / demand)
   D. Unanimous consent

   *Answer:* Two-thirds vote. See RONR (12th ed.) §15:17 — “The reason is that the two-thirds vote necessary for the adoption of any motion to modify the limits of debate also fulfills the requirement for suspending the rules (25).”

**30.** `[K0030 · Debatability]` Is the motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate debatable?
   A. Debatable
   B. Not debatable

   *Answer:* Not debatable. See RONR (12th ed.) §15:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate is one of the two motions by means of which an assembly can exercise special control over debate on a pending question or on a series of pending questions.”

**31.** `[K0031 · Second required]` Does the motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate require a second?
   A. Requires a second
   B. No second required

   *Answer:* Requires a second. See RONR (12th ed.) §15:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate is one of the two motions by means of which an assembly can exercise special control over debate on a pending question or on a series of pending questions.”

**32.** `[K0032 · Amendability]` Can the motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate be amended?
   A. Amendable
   B. Not amendable

   *Answer:* Amendable. See RONR (12th ed.) §15:6 — “Any relevant provisions can be included in the motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate by its maker or, while the motion is pending, can be added or modified by amendment (12).”

**33.** `[K0033 · Vote required]` What vote does the motion to Postpone to a Certain Time require to be adopted?
   A. Majority vote
   B. Two-thirds vote
   C. No vote (chair rules / demand)
   D. Unanimous consent

   *Answer:* Majority vote. See RONR (12th ed.) §14:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Postpone to a Certain Time (or Postpone Definitely, or Postpone) is the motion by which action on a pending question can be put off, within limits, to a definite session, day, meeting, or hour, or until after a certain event.”

**34.** `[K0034 · Debatability]` Is the motion to Postpone to a Certain Time debatable?
   A. Debatable
   B. Not debatable

   *Answer:* Debatable. See RONR (12th ed.) §14:1 — “(The expression “to defer” should be avoided, since it is often subject to vague usage.) This motion can be moved regardless of how much debate there has been on the motion it proposes to postpone.”

**35.** `[K0035 · Second required]` Does the motion to Postpone to a Certain Time require a second?
   A. Requires a second
   B. No second required

   *Answer:* Requires a second. See RONR (12th ed.) §14:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Postpone to a Certain Time (or Postpone Definitely, or Postpone) is the motion by which action on a pending question can be put off, within limits, to a definite session, day, meeting, or hour, or until after a certain event.”

**36.** `[K0036 · Amendability]` Can the motion to Postpone to a Certain Time be amended?
   A. Amendable
   B. Not amendable

   *Answer:* Amendable. See RONR (12th ed.) §14:3 — “If the effect would be to change action already taken by the assembly, as, for example, “to postpone for three weeks the dinner scheduled for October 15,” such a motion is a particular case of the motion to Amend Something Previously Adopted (35).”

**37.** `[K0037 · Vote required]` What vote does the motion to Commit or Refer require to be adopted?
   A. Majority vote
   B. Two-thirds vote
   C. No vote (chair rules / demand)
   D. Unanimous consent

   *Answer:* Majority vote. See RONR (12th ed.) §13:13 — “The first of these methods of selection that receives a majority vote is then inserted into the motion to Commit and the remainder are ignored.”

**38.** `[K0038 · Debatability]` Is the motion to Commit or Refer debatable?
   A. Debatable
   B. Not debatable

   *Answer:* Debatable. See RONR (12th ed.) §13:14 — “As soon as it is completed, if it is a pending motion to Commit, the chair states the question on it, thus opening it to additional debate during which any member can move—or the chair himself can suggest—that it be amended by adding instructions.”

**39.** `[K0039 · Second required]` Does the motion to Commit or Refer require a second?
   A. Requires a second
   B. No second required

   *Answer:* Requires a second. See RONR (12th ed.) §13:4 — “The term recommit is applied to a motion that proposes to refer a question a second time, either to the same committee that previously considered it or to a different one.”

**40.** `[K0040 · Amendability]` Can the motion to Commit or Refer be amended?
   A. Amendable
   B. Not amendable

   *Answer:* Amendable. See RONR (12th ed.) §13:14 — “As soon as it is completed, if it is a pending motion to Commit, the chair states the question on it, thus opening it to additional debate during which any member can move—or the chair himself can suggest—that it be amended by adding instructions.”

**41.** `[K0041 · Vote required]` What vote does the motion to Amend require to be adopted?
   A. Majority vote
   B. Two-thirds vote
   C. No vote (chair rules / demand)
   D. Unanimous consent

   *Answer:* Majority vote. See RONR (12th ed.) §12:94 — “When debate on the suggestions has ended (or, if the pending motion is undebatable, when no more suggestions are offered), the assembly decides, by majority vote, which suggestion(s) shall fill the blank.”

**42.** `[K0042 · Debatability]` Is the motion to Amend debatable?
   A. Debatable
   B. Not debatable

   *Answer:* Debatable. See RONR (12th ed.) §12:21 — “[Debate, if any, provided that debate is in order.] The question is on whether the amendment is germane to the resolution [or “to the primary amendment”].”

**43.** `[K0043 · Second required]` Does the motion to Amend require a second?
   A. Requires a second
   B. No second required

   *Answer:* Requires a second. See RONR (12th ed.) §12:11 — “As noted in Standard Characteristics 2 and 6, above, a subsidiary motion to Amend can, in general, be amended, so that two degrees of amendment—primary and secondary—are possible.”

**44.** `[K0044 · Amendability]` Can the motion to Amend be amended?
   A. Amendable
   B. Not amendable

   *Answer:* Amendable. See RONR (12th ed.) §12:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Amend is a motion to modify the wording—and within certain limits the meaning—of a pending motion before the pending motion itself is acted upon.”

**45.** `[K0045 · Vote required]` What vote does the motion to Postpone Indefinitely require to be adopted?
   A. Majority vote
   B. Two-thirds vote
   C. No vote (chair rules / demand)
   D. Unanimous consent

   *Answer:* Majority vote. See RONR (12th ed.) §11:1 — “Postpone Indefinitely is a motion that the assembly decline to take a position on the main question.”

**46.** `[K0046 · Debatability]` Is the motion to Postpone Indefinitely debatable?
   A. Debatable
   B. Not debatable

   *Answer:* Debatable. See RONR (12th ed.) §11:7 — “Debate on the subsidiary motion to Postpone Indefinitely will likely involve also the advisability of the resolution itself.”

**47.** `[K0047 · Second required]` Does the motion to Postpone Indefinitely require a second?
   A. Requires a second
   B. No second required

   *Answer:* Requires a second. See RONR (12th ed.) §11:1 — “Postpone Indefinitely is a motion that the assembly decline to take a position on the main question.”

**48.** `[K0048 · Amendability]` Can the motion to Postpone Indefinitely be amended?
   A. Amendable
   B. Not amendable

   *Answer:* Not amendable. See RONR (12th ed.) §11:1 — “Postpone Indefinitely is a motion that the assembly decline to take a position on the main question.”

**49.** `[K0073 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Adjourn
   B. Recess
   C. Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn

   *Answer:* Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:2 — “A motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn is in order only if at the time it is offered there is no meeting scheduled for later within the same session.”

**50.** `[K0074 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn
   B. Adjourn
   C. Recess

   *Answer:* Recess. See RONR (12th ed.) §20:1 — “A recess is a short intermission in the assembly’s proceedings, commonly of only a few minutes, which does not close the meeting and after which business will immediately be resumed at exactly the point where it was interrupted.”

**51.** `[K0075 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence (top = higher, bottom = lower).

   *Answer:* Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn → Adjourn → Recess. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:2 — “A motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn is in order only if at the time it is offered there is no meeting scheduled for later within the same session.”

**52.** `[K0076 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Adjourn
   B. Recess
   C. Raise a Question of Privilege

   *Answer:* Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §21:1 — “To adjourn means to close the meeting (8).”

**53.** `[K0077 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Adjourn
   B. Recess
   C. Raise a Question of Privilege

   *Answer:* Raise a Question of Privilege. See RONR (12th ed.) §19:3 — “The “raising” of a question of privilege is governed by rules appropriate to the device’s high rank in the order of precedence of motions.”

**54.** `[K0078 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence (top = higher, bottom = lower).

   *Answer:* Adjourn → Recess → Raise a Question of Privilege. See RONR (12th ed.) §21:1 — “To adjourn means to close the meeting (8).”

**55.** `[K0079 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Call for the Orders of the Day
   B. Raise a Question of Privilege
   C. Recess

   *Answer:* Recess. See RONR (12th ed.) §20:1 — “A recess is a short intermission in the assembly’s proceedings, commonly of only a few minutes, which does not close the meeting and after which business will immediately be resumed at exactly the point where it was interrupted.”

**56.** `[K0080 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Raise a Question of Privilege
   B. Recess
   C. Call for the Orders of the Day

   *Answer:* Call for the Orders of the Day. See RONR (12th ed.) §18:2 — “Taking up business in the prescribed order is of substantial importance, especially in conventions—which must follow a closely regulated schedule with much of the underlying work taking place off the convention floor in conferences and committees.”

**57.** `[K0081 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence (top = higher, bottom = lower).

   *Answer:* Recess → Raise a Question of Privilege → Call for the Orders of the Day. See RONR (12th ed.) §20:1 — “A recess is a short intermission in the assembly’s proceedings, commonly of only a few minutes, which does not close the meeting and after which business will immediately be resumed at exactly the point where it was interrupted.”

**58.** `[K0082 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Call for the Orders of the Day
   B. Lay on the Table
   C. Raise a Question of Privilege

   *Answer:* Raise a Question of Privilege. See RONR (12th ed.) §19:3 — “The “raising” of a question of privilege is governed by rules appropriate to the device’s high rank in the order of precedence of motions.”

**59.** `[K0083 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Call for the Orders of the Day
   B. Lay on the Table
   C. Raise a Question of Privilege

   *Answer:* Lay on the Table. See RONR (12th ed.) §17:2 — “By adopting the motion to Lay on the Table, a majority has the power to halt consideration of a question immediately without debate.”

**60.** `[K0084 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence (top = higher, bottom = lower).

   *Answer:* Raise a Question of Privilege → Call for the Orders of the Day → Lay on the Table. See RONR (12th ed.) §19:3 — “The “raising” of a question of privilege is governed by rules appropriate to the device’s high rank in the order of precedence of motions.”

**61.** `[K0085 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Previous Question
   B. Lay on the Table
   C. Call for the Orders of the Day

   *Answer:* Call for the Orders of the Day. See RONR (12th ed.) §18:2 — “Taking up business in the prescribed order is of substantial importance, especially in conventions—which must follow a closely regulated schedule with much of the underlying work taking place off the convention floor in conferences and committees.”

**62.** `[K0086 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Lay on the Table
   B. Previous Question
   C. Call for the Orders of the Day

   *Answer:* Previous Question. See RONR (12th ed.) §16:1 — “The Previous Question is the motion used to bring the assembly to an immediate vote on one or more pending questions;”

**63.** `[K0087 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence (top = higher, bottom = lower).

   *Answer:* Call for the Orders of the Day → Lay on the Table → Previous Question. See RONR (12th ed.) §18:2 — “Taking up business in the prescribed order is of substantial importance, especially in conventions—which must follow a closely regulated schedule with much of the underlying work taking place off the convention floor in conferences and committees.”

**64.** `[K0088 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Lay on the Table
   B. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate
   C. Previous Question

   *Answer:* Lay on the Table. See RONR (12th ed.) §17:2 — “By adopting the motion to Lay on the Table, a majority has the power to halt consideration of a question immediately without debate.”

**65.** `[K0089 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Previous Question
   B. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate
   C. Lay on the Table

   *Answer:* Limit or Extend Limits of Debate. See RONR (12th ed.) §15:6 — “This motion’s effect upon other pending and subsequent motions is closely related to its position in the order of precedence of motions (5) and further depends on the nature of its specific provisions, as explained below.”

**66.** `[K0090 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence (top = higher, bottom = lower).

   *Answer:* Lay on the Table → Previous Question → Limit or Extend Limits of Debate. See RONR (12th ed.) §17:2 — “By adopting the motion to Lay on the Table, a majority has the power to halt consideration of a question immediately without debate.”

**67.** `[K0091 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Postpone to a Certain Time
   B. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate
   C. Previous Question

   *Answer:* Previous Question. See RONR (12th ed.) §16:1 — “The Previous Question is the motion used to bring the assembly to an immediate vote on one or more pending questions;”

**68.** `[K0092 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate
   B. Previous Question
   C. Postpone to a Certain Time

   *Answer:* Postpone to a Certain Time. See RONR (12th ed.) §14:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Postpone to a Certain Time (or Postpone Definitely, or Postpone) is the motion by which action on a pending question can be put off, within limits, to a definite session, day, meeting, or hour, or until after a certain event.”

**69.** `[K0093 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence (top = higher, bottom = lower).

   *Answer:* Previous Question → Limit or Extend Limits of Debate → Postpone to a Certain Time. See RONR (12th ed.) §16:1 — “The Previous Question is the motion used to bring the assembly to an immediate vote on one or more pending questions;”

**70.** `[K0094 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Commit or Refer
   B. Postpone to a Certain Time
   C. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate

   *Answer:* Limit or Extend Limits of Debate. See RONR (12th ed.) §15:6 — “This motion’s effect upon other pending and subsequent motions is closely related to its position in the order of precedence of motions (5) and further depends on the nature of its specific provisions, as explained below.”

**71.** `[K0095 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate
   B. Commit or Refer
   C. Postpone to a Certain Time

   *Answer:* Commit or Refer. See RONR (12th ed.) §13:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Commit or Refer is generally used to send a pending question to a relatively small group of selected persons—a committee—so that the question may be carefully investigated and put into better condition for the assembly to consider.”

**72.** `[K0096 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence (top = higher, bottom = lower).

   *Answer:* Limit or Extend Limits of Debate → Postpone to a Certain Time → Commit or Refer. See RONR (12th ed.) §15:6 — “This motion’s effect upon other pending and subsequent motions is closely related to its position in the order of precedence of motions (5) and further depends on the nature of its specific provisions, as explained below.”

**73.** `[K0097 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Commit or Refer
   B. Amend
   C. Postpone to a Certain Time

   *Answer:* Postpone to a Certain Time. See RONR (12th ed.) §14:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Postpone to a Certain Time (or Postpone Definitely, or Postpone) is the motion by which action on a pending question can be put off, within limits, to a definite session, day, meeting, or hour, or until after a certain event.”

**74.** `[K0098 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Commit or Refer
   B. Amend
   C. Postpone to a Certain Time

   *Answer:* Amend. See RONR (12th ed.) §12:96 — “As soon as a member has made a suggestion (or, if the member had obtained the floor, as soon as the member has yielded the floor), the chair repeats the member’s suggestion and calls for further suggestions.”

**75.** `[K0099 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence (top = higher, bottom = lower).

   *Answer:* Postpone to a Certain Time → Commit or Refer → Amend. See RONR (12th ed.) §14:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Postpone to a Certain Time (or Postpone Definitely, or Postpone) is the motion by which action on a pending question can be put off, within limits, to a definite session, day, meeting, or hour, or until after a certain event.”

**76.** `[K0100 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Amend
   B. Postpone Indefinitely
   C. Commit or Refer

   *Answer:* Commit or Refer. See RONR (12th ed.) §13:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Commit or Refer is generally used to send a pending question to a relatively small group of selected persons—a committee—so that the question may be carefully investigated and put into better condition for the assembly to consider.”

**77.** `[K0101 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Postpone Indefinitely
   B. Amend
   C. Commit or Refer

   *Answer:* Postpone Indefinitely. See RONR (12th ed.) §11:1 — “Postpone Indefinitely is a motion that the assembly decline to take a position on the main question.”

**78.** `[K0102 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence (top = higher, bottom = lower).

   *Answer:* Commit or Refer → Amend → Postpone Indefinitely. See RONR (12th ed.) §13:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Commit or Refer is generally used to send a pending question to a relatively small group of selected persons—a committee—so that the question may be carefully investigated and put into better condition for the assembly to consider.”

**79.** `[K0103 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn
   B. Raise a Question of Privilege
   C. Recess
   D. Adjourn

   *Answer:* Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:2 — “A motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn is in order only if at the time it is offered there is no meeting scheduled for later within the same session.”

**80.** `[K0104 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Raise a Question of Privilege
   B. Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn
   C. Adjourn
   D. Recess

   *Answer:* Raise a Question of Privilege. See RONR (12th ed.) §19:3 — “The “raising” of a question of privilege is governed by rules appropriate to the device’s high rank in the order of precedence of motions.”

**81.** `[K0105 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence (top = higher, bottom = lower).

   *Answer:* Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn → Adjourn → Recess → Raise a Question of Privilege. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:2 — “A motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn is in order only if at the time it is offered there is no meeting scheduled for later within the same session.”

**82.** `[K0106 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Raise a Question of Privilege
   B. Adjourn
   C. Call for the Orders of the Day
   D. Recess

   *Answer:* Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §21:1 — “To adjourn means to close the meeting (8).”

**83.** `[K0107 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Adjourn
   B. Raise a Question of Privilege
   C. Recess
   D. Call for the Orders of the Day

   *Answer:* Call for the Orders of the Day. See RONR (12th ed.) §18:2 — “Taking up business in the prescribed order is of substantial importance, especially in conventions—which must follow a closely regulated schedule with much of the underlying work taking place off the convention floor in conferences and committees.”

**84.** `[K0108 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence (top = higher, bottom = lower).

   *Answer:* Adjourn → Recess → Raise a Question of Privilege → Call for the Orders of the Day. See RONR (12th ed.) §21:1 — “To adjourn means to close the meeting (8).”

**85.** `[K0109 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Call for the Orders of the Day
   B. Raise a Question of Privilege
   C. Lay on the Table
   D. Recess

   *Answer:* Recess. See RONR (12th ed.) §20:1 — “A recess is a short intermission in the assembly’s proceedings, commonly of only a few minutes, which does not close the meeting and after which business will immediately be resumed at exactly the point where it was interrupted.”

**86.** `[K0110 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Lay on the Table
   B. Recess
   C. Raise a Question of Privilege
   D. Call for the Orders of the Day

   *Answer:* Lay on the Table. See RONR (12th ed.) §17:2 — “By adopting the motion to Lay on the Table, a majority has the power to halt consideration of a question immediately without debate.”

**87.** `[K0111 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence (top = higher, bottom = lower).

   *Answer:* Recess → Raise a Question of Privilege → Call for the Orders of the Day → Lay on the Table. See RONR (12th ed.) §20:1 — “A recess is a short intermission in the assembly’s proceedings, commonly of only a few minutes, which does not close the meeting and after which business will immediately be resumed at exactly the point where it was interrupted.”

**88.** `[K0112 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Raise a Question of Privilege
   B. Call for the Orders of the Day
   C. Previous Question
   D. Lay on the Table

   *Answer:* Raise a Question of Privilege. See RONR (12th ed.) §19:3 — “The “raising” of a question of privilege is governed by rules appropriate to the device’s high rank in the order of precedence of motions.”

**89.** `[K0113 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Lay on the Table
   B. Raise a Question of Privilege
   C. Previous Question
   D. Call for the Orders of the Day

   *Answer:* Previous Question. See RONR (12th ed.) §16:1 — “The Previous Question is the motion used to bring the assembly to an immediate vote on one or more pending questions;”

**90.** `[K0114 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence (top = higher, bottom = lower).

   *Answer:* Raise a Question of Privilege → Call for the Orders of the Day → Lay on the Table → Previous Question. See RONR (12th ed.) §19:3 — “The “raising” of a question of privilege is governed by rules appropriate to the device’s high rank in the order of precedence of motions.”

**91.** `[K0115 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Lay on the Table
   B. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate
   C. Previous Question
   D. Call for the Orders of the Day

   *Answer:* Call for the Orders of the Day. See RONR (12th ed.) §18:2 — “Taking up business in the prescribed order is of substantial importance, especially in conventions—which must follow a closely regulated schedule with much of the underlying work taking place off the convention floor in conferences and committees.”

**92.** `[K0116 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate
   B. Call for the Orders of the Day
   C. Previous Question
   D. Lay on the Table

   *Answer:* Limit or Extend Limits of Debate. See RONR (12th ed.) §15:6 — “This motion’s effect upon other pending and subsequent motions is closely related to its position in the order of precedence of motions (5) and further depends on the nature of its specific provisions, as explained below.”

**93.** `[K0117 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence (top = higher, bottom = lower).

   *Answer:* Call for the Orders of the Day → Lay on the Table → Previous Question → Limit or Extend Limits of Debate. See RONR (12th ed.) §18:2 — “Taking up business in the prescribed order is of substantial importance, especially in conventions—which must follow a closely regulated schedule with much of the underlying work taking place off the convention floor in conferences and committees.”

**94.** `[K0118 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate
   B. Previous Question
   C. Lay on the Table
   D. Postpone to a Certain Time

   *Answer:* Lay on the Table. See RONR (12th ed.) §17:2 — “By adopting the motion to Lay on the Table, a majority has the power to halt consideration of a question immediately without debate.”

**95.** `[K0119 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Postpone to a Certain Time
   B. Lay on the Table
   C. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate
   D. Previous Question

   *Answer:* Postpone to a Certain Time. See RONR (12th ed.) §14:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Postpone to a Certain Time (or Postpone Definitely, or Postpone) is the motion by which action on a pending question can be put off, within limits, to a definite session, day, meeting, or hour, or until after a certain event.”

**96.** `[K0120 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence (top = higher, bottom = lower).

   *Answer:* Lay on the Table → Previous Question → Limit or Extend Limits of Debate → Postpone to a Certain Time. See RONR (12th ed.) §17:2 — “By adopting the motion to Lay on the Table, a majority has the power to halt consideration of a question immediately without debate.”

**97.** `[K0121 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Commit or Refer
   B. Postpone to a Certain Time
   C. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate
   D. Previous Question

   *Answer:* Previous Question. See RONR (12th ed.) §16:1 — “The Previous Question is the motion used to bring the assembly to an immediate vote on one or more pending questions;”

**98.** `[K0122 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate
   B. Postpone to a Certain Time
   C. Previous Question
   D. Commit or Refer

   *Answer:* Commit or Refer. See RONR (12th ed.) §13:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Commit or Refer is generally used to send a pending question to a relatively small group of selected persons—a committee—so that the question may be carefully investigated and put into better condition for the assembly to consider.”

**99.** `[K0123 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence (top = higher, bottom = lower).

   *Answer:* Previous Question → Limit or Extend Limits of Debate → Postpone to a Certain Time → Commit or Refer. See RONR (12th ed.) §16:1 — “The Previous Question is the motion used to bring the assembly to an immediate vote on one or more pending questions;”

**100.** `[K0124 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate
   B. Postpone to a Certain Time
   C. Commit or Refer
   D. Amend

   *Answer:* Limit or Extend Limits of Debate. See RONR (12th ed.) §15:6 — “This motion’s effect upon other pending and subsequent motions is closely related to its position in the order of precedence of motions (5) and further depends on the nature of its specific provisions, as explained below.”

**101.** `[K0125 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Amend
   B. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate
   C. Commit or Refer
   D. Postpone to a Certain Time

   *Answer:* Amend. See RONR (12th ed.) §12:96 — “As soon as a member has made a suggestion (or, if the member had obtained the floor, as soon as the member has yielded the floor), the chair repeats the member’s suggestion and calls for further suggestions.”

**102.** `[K0126 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence (top = higher, bottom = lower).

   *Answer:* Limit or Extend Limits of Debate → Postpone to a Certain Time → Commit or Refer → Amend. See RONR (12th ed.) §15:6 — “This motion’s effect upon other pending and subsequent motions is closely related to its position in the order of precedence of motions (5) and further depends on the nature of its specific provisions, as explained below.”

**103.** `[K0127 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Amend
   B. Postpone Indefinitely
   C. Postpone to a Certain Time
   D. Commit or Refer

   *Answer:* Postpone to a Certain Time. See RONR (12th ed.) §14:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Postpone to a Certain Time (or Postpone Definitely, or Postpone) is the motion by which action on a pending question can be put off, within limits, to a definite session, day, meeting, or hour, or until after a certain event.”

**104.** `[K0128 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Amend
   B. Postpone Indefinitely
   C. Commit or Refer
   D. Postpone to a Certain Time

   *Answer:* Postpone Indefinitely. See RONR (12th ed.) §11:1 — “Postpone Indefinitely is a motion that the assembly decline to take a position on the main question.”

**105.** `[K0129 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence (top = higher, bottom = lower).

   *Answer:* Postpone to a Certain Time → Commit or Refer → Amend → Postpone Indefinitely. See RONR (12th ed.) §14:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Postpone to a Certain Time (or Postpone Definitely, or Postpone) is the motion by which action on a pending question can be put off, within limits, to a definite session, day, meeting, or hour, or until after a certain event.”

**106.** `[K0130 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Recess
   B. Call for the Orders of the Day
   C. Raise a Question of Privilege
   D. Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn
   E. Adjourn

   *Answer:* Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:2 — “A motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn is in order only if at the time it is offered there is no meeting scheduled for later within the same session.”

**107.** `[K0131 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Raise a Question of Privilege
   B. Adjourn
   C. Call for the Orders of the Day
   D. Recess
   E. Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn

   *Answer:* Call for the Orders of the Day. See RONR (12th ed.) §18:2 — “Taking up business in the prescribed order is of substantial importance, especially in conventions—which must follow a closely regulated schedule with much of the underlying work taking place off the convention floor in conferences and committees.”

**108.** `[K0132 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence (top = higher, bottom = lower).

   *Answer:* Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn → Adjourn → Recess → Raise a Question of Privilege → Call for the Orders of the Day. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:2 — “A motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn is in order only if at the time it is offered there is no meeting scheduled for later within the same session.”

**109.** `[K0133 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Lay on the Table
   B. Recess
   C. Call for the Orders of the Day
   D. Adjourn
   E. Raise a Question of Privilege

   *Answer:* Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §21:1 — “To adjourn means to close the meeting (8).”

**110.** `[K0134 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Raise a Question of Privilege
   B. Recess
   C. Adjourn
   D. Call for the Orders of the Day
   E. Lay on the Table

   *Answer:* Lay on the Table. See RONR (12th ed.) §17:2 — “By adopting the motion to Lay on the Table, a majority has the power to halt consideration of a question immediately without debate.”

**111.** `[K0135 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence (top = higher, bottom = lower).

   *Answer:* Adjourn → Recess → Raise a Question of Privilege → Call for the Orders of the Day → Lay on the Table. See RONR (12th ed.) §21:1 — “To adjourn means to close the meeting (8).”

**112.** `[K0136 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Raise a Question of Privilege
   B. Call for the Orders of the Day
   C. Lay on the Table
   D. Recess
   E. Previous Question

   *Answer:* Recess. See RONR (12th ed.) §20:1 — “A recess is a short intermission in the assembly’s proceedings, commonly of only a few minutes, which does not close the meeting and after which business will immediately be resumed at exactly the point where it was interrupted.”

**113.** `[K0137 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Recess
   B. Lay on the Table
   C. Call for the Orders of the Day
   D. Previous Question
   E. Raise a Question of Privilege

   *Answer:* Previous Question. See RONR (12th ed.) §16:1 — “The Previous Question is the motion used to bring the assembly to an immediate vote on one or more pending questions;”

**114.** `[K0138 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence (top = higher, bottom = lower).

   *Answer:* Recess → Raise a Question of Privilege → Call for the Orders of the Day → Lay on the Table → Previous Question. See RONR (12th ed.) §20:1 — “A recess is a short intermission in the assembly’s proceedings, commonly of only a few minutes, which does not close the meeting and after which business will immediately be resumed at exactly the point where it was interrupted.”

**115.** `[K0139 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Call for the Orders of the Day
   B. Previous Question
   C. Raise a Question of Privilege
   D. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate
   E. Lay on the Table

   *Answer:* Raise a Question of Privilege. See RONR (12th ed.) §19:3 — “The “raising” of a question of privilege is governed by rules appropriate to the device’s high rank in the order of precedence of motions.”

**116.** `[K0140 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Call for the Orders of the Day
   B. Raise a Question of Privilege

   E. Previous Question

**117.** `[K0141 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence (top = higher, bottom = lower).

   *Answer:* Raise a Question of Privilege → Call for the Orders of the Day → Lay on the Table → Previous Question → Limit or Extend Limits of Debate.
