**103.** `[C0029 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: For these reasons, a corporate charter generally should contain only what is necessary to obtain it, and to establish the desired status of the organization under law—leaving as much as possible to the bylaws or to _____ rules if appropriate in accordance with the principles explained below and in 56.

   *Answer:* lower-ranking — “For these reasons, a corporate charter generally should contain only what is necessary to obtain it, and to establish the desired status of the organization under law—leaving as much as possible to the bylaws or to lower-ranking rules if appropriate in accordance with the principles explained below and in 56.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:7 — “For these reasons, a corporate charter generally should contain only what is necessary to obtain it, and to establish the desired status of the organization under law—leaving as much as possible to the bylaws or to lower-ranking rules if appropriate in accordance with the principles explained below and in 56.”

**104.** `[C0029 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: For these reasons, a corporate charter generally should contain only what is necessary to obtain it, and to establish the desired status of the organization under law—leaving as much as possible to the bylaws or to _____ rules if appropriate in accordance with the principles explained below and in 56.
   A. Organization
   B. lower-ranking
   C. presentation
   D. President-Elect

   *Answer:* B) lower-ranking. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:7 — “For these reasons, a corporate charter generally should contain only what is necessary to obtain it, and to establish the desired status of the organization under law—leaving as much as possible to the bylaws or to lower-ranking rules if appropriate in accordance with the principles explained below and in 56.”

**105.** `[C0030 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: In general, the constitution or the _____—or both—of a society are the documents that contain its own basic rules relating principally to itself as an organization, rather than to the parliamentary procedure that it follows.

   *Answer:* bylaws — “In general, the constitution or the bylaws—or both—of a society are the documents that contain its own basic rules relating principally to itself as an organization, rather than to the parliamentary procedure that it follows.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:8 — “In general, the constitution or the bylaws—or both—of a society are the documents that contain its own basic rules relating principally to itself as an organization, rather than to the parliamentary procedure that it follows.”

**106.** `[C0030 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: In general, the constitution or the bylaws—or both—of a society are the documents that contain its own basic rules relating principally to itself as an organization, rather than to the _____ procedure that it follows.
   A. requirement
   B. representatives
   C. constitution
   D. parliamentary

   *Answer:* D) parliamentary. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:8 — “In general, the constitution or the bylaws—or both—of a society are the documents that contain its own basic rules relating principally to itself as an organization, rather than to the parliamentary procedure that it follows.”

**107.** `[C0030 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The term bylaws, as used in this book, refers to this single, _____ instrument—by whatever name the particular organization may describe it—which:
   A. adjournment
   B. Parliamentary
   C. combination-type
   D. consideration

   *Answer:* C) combination-type. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:8 — “The term bylaws, as used in this book, refers to this single, combination-type instrument—by whatever name the particular organization may describe it—which:”

**108.** `[C0030 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The term bylaws, as used in this book, refers to this single, _____ instrument—by whatever name the particular organization may describe it—which:

   *Answer:* combination-type — “The term bylaws, as used in this book, refers to this single, combination-type instrument—by whatever name the particular organization may describe it—which:”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:8 — “The term bylaws, as used in this book, refers to this single, combination-type instrument—by whatever name the particular organization may describe it—which:”

**109.** `[C0031 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: While the number of articles in the bylaws will be determined by the size and activities of the organization, the general nature of the subjects covered will be indicated by the following list of articles, typical of those found in the bylaws of the average _____ society: (1) Name of the organization;

   *Answer:* unincorporated — “While the number of articles in the bylaws will be determined by the size and activities of the organization, the general nature of the subjects covered will be indicated by the following list of articles, typical of those found in the bylaws of the average unincorporated society: (1) Name of the organization;”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:9 — “While the number of articles in the bylaws will be determined by the size and activities of the organization, the general nature of the subjects covered will be indicated by the following list of articles, typical of those found in the bylaws of the average unincorporated society: (1) Name of the organization;”

**110.** `[C0031 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: (8) _____ Authority (that is, the name of the manual of parliamentary procedure that the organization is to follow;
   A. president-elect
   B. resolutions
   C. Parliamentary
   D. subsequently

   *Answer:* C) Parliamentary. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:9 — “(8) Parliamentary Authority (that is, the name of the manual of parliamentary procedure that the organization is to follow;”

**111.** `[C0031 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: and (9) Amendment of Bylaws (_____ the procedure for making changes in the bylaws).

   *Answer:* prescribing — “and (9) Amendment of Bylaws (prescribing the procedure for making changes in the bylaws).”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:9 — “and (9) Amendment of Bylaws (prescribing the procedure for making changes in the bylaws).”

**112.** `[C0031 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: If the society is _____, its name and its object are usually set forth in the corporate charter, in which case the first two articles listed above should be omitted from the bylaws.
   A. specification
   B. deliberative
   C. requirement
   D. incorporated

   *Answer:* D) incorporated. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:9 — “If the society is incorporated, its name and its object are usually set forth in the corporate charter, in which case the first two articles listed above should be omitted from the bylaws.”

**113.** `[C0031 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The _____ content of bylaws is discussed in detail in 56.

   *Answer:* appropriate — “The appropriate content of bylaws is discussed in detail in 56.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:9 — “The appropriate content of bylaws is discussed in detail in 56.”

**114.** `[C0032 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: It formerly was common practice to divide the basic rules of an organization into two documents, in order that one of them —the _____—might be made more difficult to amend than the other, to which the name bylaws was applied.

   *Answer:* constitution — “It formerly was common practice to divide the basic rules of an organization into two documents, in order that one of them —the constitution—might be made more difficult to amend than the other, to which the name bylaws was applied.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:10 — “It formerly was common practice to divide the basic rules of an organization into two documents, in order that one of them —the constitution—might be made more difficult to amend than the other, to which the name bylaws was applied.”

**115.** `[C0032 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: It formerly was common practice to divide the basic rules of an organization into two documents, in order that one of them —the constitution—might be made more difficult to amend than the other, to which the name _____ was applied.
   A. adopts
   B. action
   C. adhere
   D. bylaws

   *Answer:* D) bylaws. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:10 — “It formerly was common practice to divide the basic rules of an organization into two documents, in order that one of them —the constitution—might be made more difficult to amend than the other, to which the name bylaws was applied.”

**116.** `[C0032 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Such an arrangement may still be found in cases where a national, state, or local law applying to the particular type of organization requires a constitution separate from the bylaws, or in older _____ that have had little occasion to change their existing rules.
   A. organizations
   B. presentation
   C. immediately
   D. undebatable

   *Answer:* A) organizations. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:10 — “Such an arrangement may still be found in cases where a national, state, or local law applying to the particular type of organization requires a constitution separate from the bylaws, or in older organizations that have had little occasion to change their existing rules.”

**117.** `[C0032 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Such an arrangement may still be found in cases where a national, state, or local law applying to the particular type of organization requires a constitution separate from the bylaws, or in older _____ that have had little occasion to change their existing rules.

   *Answer:* organizations — “Such an arrangement may still be found in cases where a national, state, or local law applying to the particular type of organization requires a constitution separate from the bylaws, or in older organizations that have had little occasion to change their existing rules.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:10 — “Such an arrangement may still be found in cases where a national, state, or local law applying to the particular type of organization requires a constitution separate from the bylaws, or in older organizations that have had little occasion to change their existing rules.”

**118.** `[C0033 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: In an _____ society there generally should not be a constitution separate from the bylaws, since in such a case the constitution would duplicate much of the corporate charter.

   *Answer:* incorporated — “In an incorporated society there generally should not be a constitution separate from the bylaws, since in such a case the constitution would duplicate much of the corporate charter.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:11 — “In an incorporated society there generally should not be a constitution separate from the bylaws, since in such a case the constitution would duplicate much of the corporate charter.”

**119.** `[C0033 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Although it is not improper, in an _____ society, to have both a constitution and bylaws as separate documents (provided that the constitution is made more difficult to amend), there are decided advantages in keeping all of the provisions relating to each subject under one heading within a single instrument—which results in fewer problems of duplication or inconsistency, and gives a more understandable and workable body of rules.
   A. deliberative
   B. constitution
   C. unincorporated
   D. recommendation

   *Answer:* C) unincorporated. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:11 — “Although it is not improper, in an unincorporated society, to have both a constitution and bylaws as separate documents (provided that the constitution is made more difficult to amend), there are decided advantages in keeping all of the provisions relating to each subject under one heading within a single instrument—which results in fewer problems of duplication or inconsistency, and gives a more understandable and workable body of rules.”

**120.** `[C0033 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: In an _____ society there generally should not be a constitution separate from the bylaws, since in such a case the constitution would duplicate much of the corporate charter.
   A. resolutions
   B. Permission
   C. incorporated
   D. Information

   *Answer:* C) incorporated. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:11 — “In an incorporated society there generally should not be a constitution separate from the bylaws, since in such a case the constitution would duplicate much of the corporate charter.”

**121.** `[C0033 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Although it is not improper, in an _____ society, to have both a constitution and bylaws as separate documents (provided that the constitution is made more difficult to amend), there are decided advantages in keeping all of the provisions relating to each subject under one heading within a single instrument—which results in fewer problems of duplication or inconsistency, and gives a more understandable and workable body of rules.

   *Answer:* unincorporated — “Although it is not improper, in an unincorporated society, to have both a constitution and bylaws as separate documents (provided that the constitution is made more difficult to amend), there are decided advantages in keeping all of the provisions relating to each subject under one heading within a single instrument—which results in fewer problems of duplication or inconsistency, and gives a more understandable and workable body of rules.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:11 — “Although it is not improper, in an unincorporated society, to have both a constitution and bylaws as separate documents (provided that the constitution is made more difficult to amend), there are decided advantages in keeping all of the provisions relating to each subject under one heading within a single instrument—which results in fewer problems of duplication or inconsistency, and gives a more understandable and workable body of rules.”

**122.** `[C0034 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Except for the corporate charter in an incorporated society, the bylaws (as the single, _____ instrument is called in this book) comprise the highest body of rules in societies as normally established today.

   *Answer:* combination-type — “Except for the corporate charter in an incorporated society, the bylaws (as the single, combination-type instrument is called in this book) comprise the highest body of rules in societies as normally established today.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:12 — “Except for the corporate charter in an incorporated society, the bylaws (as the single, combination-type instrument is called in this book) comprise the highest body of rules in societies as normally established today.”

**123.** `[C0034 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Except for the corporate charter in an incorporated society, the bylaws (as the single, combination-type _____ is called in this book) comprise the highest body of rules in societies as normally established today.
   A. landscaped
   B. instrument
   C. substitute
   D. resolution

   *Answer:* B) instrument. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:12 — “Except for the corporate charter in an incorporated society, the bylaws (as the single, combination-type instrument is called in this book) comprise the highest body of rules in societies as normally established today.”

**124.** `[C0034 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: In _____ that have both a constitution and bylaws as separate documents, however, the constitution is the higher of the two bodies of rules and supersedes the bylaws.

   *Answer:* organizations — “In organizations that have both a constitution and bylaws as separate documents, however, the constitution is the higher of the two bodies of rules and supersedes the bylaws.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:12 — “In organizations that have both a constitution and bylaws as separate documents, however, the constitution is the higher of the two bodies of rules and supersedes the bylaws.”

**125.** `[C0034 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: In _____ that have both a constitution and bylaws as separate documents, however, the constitution is the higher of the two bodies of rules and supersedes the bylaws.
   A. organizations
   B. resolutions
   C. Parliamentary
   D. recommendation

   *Answer:* A) organizations. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:12 — “In organizations that have both a constitution and bylaws as separate documents, however, the constitution is the higher of the two bodies of rules and supersedes the bylaws.”

**126.** `[C0035 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The bylaws, by their nature, _____ contain whatever limitations are placed on the powers of the assembly of a society (that is, the members attending a particular one of its meetings) with respect to the society as a whole.

   *Answer:* necessarily — “The bylaws, by their nature, necessarily contain whatever limitations are placed on the powers of the assembly of a society (that is, the members attending a particular one of its meetings) with respect to the society as a whole.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:13 — “The bylaws, by their nature, necessarily contain whatever limitations are placed on the powers of the assembly of a society (that is, the members attending a particular one of its meetings) with respect to the society as a whole.”

**127.** `[C0035 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Similarly, the provisions of the bylaws have direct bearing on the rights of members within the _____—whether present or absent from the assembly.
   A. Definitely
   B. organization
   C. Information
   D. Procedures

   *Answer:* B) organization. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:13 — “Similarly, the provisions of the bylaws have direct bearing on the rights of members within the organization—whether present or absent from the assembly.”

**128.** `[C0035 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: It is a good policy for every member on joining the society to be given a copy of the bylaws, printed together with the _____ charter, if there is one, and any special rules of order or standing rules that the society may have adopted as explained below.

   *Answer:* corporate — “It is a good policy for every member on joining the society to be given a copy of the bylaws, printed together with the corporate charter, if there is one, and any special rules of order or standing rules that the society may have adopted as explained below.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:13 — “It is a good policy for every member on joining the society to be given a copy of the bylaws, printed together with the corporate charter, if there is one, and any special rules of order or standing rules that the society may have adopted as explained below.”

**129.** `[C0035 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: A member should become familiar with the contents of these rules if he looks toward full _____ in the society’s affairs.
   A. Parliamentary
   B. deliberative
   C. participation
   D. requirement

   *Answer:* C) participation. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:13 — “A member should become familiar with the contents of these rules if he looks toward full participation in the society’s affairs.”

**130.** `[C0035 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The bylaws, by their nature, _____ contain whatever limitations are placed on the powers of the assembly of a society (that is, the members attending a particular one of its meetings) with respect to the society as a whole.
   A. Incidental
   B. necessarily
   C. convention
   D. undebatable

   *Answer:* B) necessarily. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:13 — “The bylaws, by their nature, necessarily contain whatever limitations are placed on the powers of the assembly of a society (that is, the members attending a particular one of its meetings) with respect to the society as a whole.”

**131.** `[C0036 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The term _____ refers to written rules of parliamentary procedure formally adopted by an assembly or an organization.

   *Answer:* rules of order — “The term rules of order refers to written rules of parliamentary procedure formally adopted by an assembly or an organization.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:14 — “The term rules of order refers to written rules of parliamentary procedure formally adopted by an assembly or an organization.”

**132.** `[C0036 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The term rules of order refers to written rules of _____ procedure formally adopted by an assembly or an organization.
   A. Organization
   B. presentation
   C. parliamentary
   D. deliberative

   *Answer:* C) parliamentary. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:14 — “The term rules of order refers to written rules of parliamentary procedure formally adopted by an assembly or an organization.”

**133.** `[C0036 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Such rules relate to the orderly _____ of business in meetings and to the duties of officers in that connection.

   *Answer:* transaction — “Such rules relate to the orderly transaction of business in meetings and to the duties of officers in that connection.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:14 — “Such rules relate to the orderly transaction of business in meetings and to the duties of officers in that connection.”

**134.** `[C0036 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The object of rules of order is to facilitate the smooth _____ of the assembly and to provide a firm basis for resolving questions of procedure that may arise.
   A. assemblies
   B. authorizes
   C. functioning
   D. requirement

   *Answer:* C) functioning. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:14 — “The object of rules of order is to facilitate the smooth functioning of the assembly and to provide a firm basis for resolving questions of procedure that may arise.”

**135.** `[C0036 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Such rules relate to the orderly _____ of business in meetings and to the duties of officers in that connection.
   A. transaction
   B. Indefinitely
   C. Unfinished
   D. Substitute

   *Answer:* A) transaction. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:14 — “Such rules relate to the orderly transaction of business in meetings and to the duties of officers in that connection.”

**136.** `[C0037 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The usual and preferable method by which an ordinary society now provides itself with suitable rules of order is therefore to place in its bylaws a provision prescribing that the current edition of a specified and generally accepted manual of parliamentary law shall be the organization’s _____, and then to adopt only such special rules of order as it finds needed to supplement or modify rules contained in that manual.

   *Answer:* parliamentary authority — “The usual and preferable method by which an ordinary society now provides itself with suitable rules of order is therefore to place in its bylaws a provision prescribing that the current edition of a specified and generally accepted manual of parliamentary law shall be the organization’s parliamentary authority, and then to adopt only such special rules of order as it finds needed to supplement or modify rules contained in that manual.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:15 — “The usual and preferable method by which an ordinary society now provides itself with suitable rules of order is therefore to place in its bylaws a provision prescribing that the current edition of a specified and generally accepted manual of parliamentary law shall be the organization’s parliamentary authority, and then to adopt only such special rules of order as it finds needed to supplement or modify rules contained in that manual.”

**137.** `[C0037 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The usual and preferable method by which an ordinary society now provides itself with suitable rules of order is therefore to place in its bylaws a provision prescribing that the current edition of a specified and generally accepted manual of parliamentary law shall be the organization’s parliamentary authority, and then to adopt only such _____ rules of order as it finds needed to supplement or modify rules contained in that manual.
   A. special
   B. Charges
   C. Suspend
   D. Example

   *Answer:* A) special. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:15 — “The usual and preferable method by which an ordinary society now provides itself with suitable rules of order is therefore to place in its bylaws a provision prescribing that the current edition of a specified and generally accepted manual of parliamentary law shall be the organization’s parliamentary authority, and then to adopt only such special rules of order as it finds needed to supplement or modify rules contained in that manual.”

**138.** `[C0037 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: In contrast to bylaws, _____ derive their proper substance largely from the general nature of the parliamentary process rather than from the circumstances of a particular assembly.

   *Answer:* rules of order — “In contrast to bylaws, rules of order derive their proper substance largely from the general nature of the parliamentary process rather than from the circumstances of a particular assembly.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:15 — “In contrast to bylaws, rules of order derive their proper substance largely from the general nature of the parliamentary process rather than from the circumstances of a particular assembly.”

**139.** `[C0037 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: In a mass meeting or a meeting of a body not yet organized, adoption of a parliamentary authority (or individual rules of order) may take place at the beginning of the meeting by _____ vote.
   A. A three-fourths vote
   B. A majority vote
   C. A two-thirds vote
   D. A unanimous (general consent) vote

   *Answer:* B) A majority vote. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:15 — “In a mass meeting or a meeting of a body not yet organized, adoption of a parliamentary authority (or individual rules of order) may take place at the beginning of the meeting by majority vote.”

**140.** `[C0037 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: In contrast to bylaws, rules of order derive their proper substance largely from the general nature of the _____ process rather than from the circumstances of a particular assembly.

   *Answer:* parliamentary — “In contrast to bylaws, rules of order derive their proper substance largely from the general nature of the parliamentary process rather than from the circumstances of a particular assembly.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:15 — “In contrast to bylaws, rules of order derive their proper substance largely from the general nature of the parliamentary process rather than from the circumstances of a particular assembly.”

**141.** `[C0038 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Special rules of order supersede any rules in the _____ authority with which they may conflict.

   *Answer:* parliamentary — “Special rules of order supersede any rules in the parliamentary authority with which they may conflict.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:16 — “Special rules of order supersede any rules in the parliamentary authority with which they may conflict.”

**142.** `[C0038 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Special rules of order supersede any rules in the _____ authority with which they may conflict.
   A. specification
   B. Announcements
   C. parliamentary
   D. Parliamentarian

   *Answer:* C) parliamentary. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:16 — “Special rules of order supersede any rules in the parliamentary authority with which they may conflict.”

**143.** `[C0040 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: In matters on which an organization’s adopted parliamentary authority is silent, provisions found in other works on parliamentary law may be _____—that is, they may carry weight in the absence of overriding reasons for following a different course—but they are not binding on the body.

   *Answer:* persuasive — “In matters on which an organization’s adopted parliamentary authority is silent, provisions found in other works on parliamentary law may be persuasive—that is, they may carry weight in the absence of overriding reasons for following a different course—but they are not binding on the body.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:18 — “In matters on which an organization’s adopted parliamentary authority is silent, provisions found in other works on parliamentary law may be persuasive—that is, they may carry weight in the absence of overriding reasons for following a different course—but they are not binding on the body.”

**144.** `[C0040 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: When a society or an assembly has adopted a particular _____ manual—such as this book—as its authority, the rules contained in that manual are binding upon it in all cases where they are not inconsistent with the bylaws (or constitution) of the body, any of its special rules of order, or any provisions of local, state, or national law applying to the particular type of organization.
   A. undebatable
   B. parliamentary
   C. Preparation
   D. resolutions

   *Answer:* B) parliamentary. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:18 — “When a society or an assembly has adopted a particular parliamentary manual—such as this book—as its authority, the rules contained in that manual are binding upon it in all cases where they are not inconsistent with the bylaws (or constitution) of the body, any of its special rules of order, or any provisions of local, state, or national law applying to the particular type of organization.”

**145.** `[C0040 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: In matters on which an organization’s adopted parliamentary authority is silent, provisions found in other works on parliamentary law may be _____—that is, they may carry weight in the absence of overriding reasons for following a different course—but they are not binding on the body.
   A. settlement
   B. persuasive
   C. Permission
   D. privileged

   *Answer:* B) persuasive. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:18 — “In matters on which an organization’s adopted parliamentary authority is silent, provisions found in other works on parliamentary law may be persuasive—that is, they may carry weight in the absence of overriding reasons for following a different course—but they are not binding on the body.”

**146.** `[C0040 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: When a society or an assembly has adopted a particular _____ manual—such as this book—as its authority, the rules contained in that manual are binding upon it in all cases where they are not inconsistent with the bylaws (or constitution) of the body, any of its special rules of order, or any provisions of local, state, or national law applying to the particular type of organization.

   *Answer:* parliamentary — “When a society or an assembly has adopted a particular parliamentary manual—such as this book—as its authority, the rules contained in that manual are binding upon it in all cases where they are not inconsistent with the bylaws (or constitution) of the body, any of its special rules of order, or any provisions of local, state, or national law applying to the particular type of organization.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:18 — “When a society or an assembly has adopted a particular parliamentary manual—such as this book—as its authority, the rules contained in that manual are binding upon it in all cases where they are not inconsistent with the bylaws (or constitution) of the body, any of its special rules of order, or any provisions of local, state, or national law applying to the particular type of organization.”

**147.** `[C0041 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Although it is unwise for an assembly or a society to attempt to function without formally adopted rules of order, a recognized _____ manual may be cited under such conditions as persuasive.

   *Answer:* parliamentary — “Although it is unwise for an assembly or a society to attempt to function without formally adopted rules of order, a recognized parliamentary manual may be cited under such conditions as persuasive.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:19 — “Although it is unwise for an assembly or a society to attempt to function without formally adopted rules of order, a recognized parliamentary manual may be cited under such conditions as persuasive.”

**148.** `[C0041 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Or, by being followed through _____ custom in an organization, a particular manual may acquire a status within the body similar to that of an adopted parliamentary authority.
   A. interrupted
   B. Information
   C. representatives
   D. longestablished

**149.** `[C0041 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Although it is unwise for an assembly or a society to attempt to function without formally adopted rules of order, a recognized _____ manual may be cited under such conditions as persuasive.
   A. interrupted
   B. Preparation
   C. immediately
   D. parliamentary

   *Answer:* D) parliamentary. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:19 — “Although it is unwise for an assembly or a society to attempt to function without formally adopted rules of order, a recognized parliamentary manual may be cited under such conditions as persuasive.”

**150.** `[C0041 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Or, by being followed through _____ custom in an organization, a particular manual may acquire a status within the body similar to that of an adopted parliamentary authority.

   *Answer:* longestablished — “Or, by being followed through longestablished custom in an organization, a particular manual may acquire a status within the body similar to that of an adopted parliamentary authority.”. **151.** `[C0042 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Any special rules of order are adopted _____ from the bylaws.

   *Answer:* separately — “Any special rules of order are adopted separately from the bylaws.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:20 — “Any special rules of order are adopted separately from the bylaws.”

**152.** `[C0042 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: It is _____ that they be printed in the same booklet with, but under a heading separate from, the bylaws.

   C. execution
   D. postponed

   *Answer:* A) advisable. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:20 — “It is advisable that they be printed in the same booklet with, but under a heading separate from, the bylaws.”

**153.** `[C0042 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Although rules in the nature of special rules of order are sometimes placed within the bylaws—as occurs most frequently when a society prescribes its own order of business—such an _____ is less desirable, since it may lead to cases of uncertainty as to whether a particular rule can be suspended.

   *Answer:* arrangement — “Although rules in the nature of special rules of order are sometimes placed within the bylaws—as occurs most frequently when a society prescribes its own order of business—such an arrangement is less desirable, since it may lead to cases of uncertainty as to whether a particular rule can be suspended.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:20 — “Although rules in the nature of special rules of order are sometimes placed within the bylaws—as occurs most frequently when a society prescribes its own order of business—such an arrangement is less desirable, since it may lead to cases of uncertainty as to whether a particular rule can be suspended.”

**154.** `[C0042 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Any special rules of order are adopted _____ from the bylaws.
   A. separately
   B. persuasive
   C. Convention
   D. incidental

   *Answer:* A) separately. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:20 — “Any special rules of order are adopted separately from the bylaws.”

**155.** `[C0042 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: It is _____ that they be printed in the same booklet with, but under a heading separate from, the bylaws.

   *Answer:* advisable — “It is advisable that they be printed in the same booklet with, but under a heading separate from, the bylaws.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:20 — “It is advisable that they be printed in the same booklet with, but under a heading separate from, the bylaws.”

**156.** `[C0043 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Rules of order—whether contained in the parliamentary authority or adopted as special rules of order—can be suspended by a _____ vote as explained in 25 (with the exceptions there specified).

   *Answer:* two-thirds — “Rules of order—whether contained in the parliamentary authority or adopted as special rules of order—can be suspended by a two-thirds vote as explained in 25 (with the exceptions there specified).”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:21 — “Rules of order—whether contained in the parliamentary authority or adopted as special rules of order—can be suspended by a two-thirds vote as explained in 25 (with the exceptions there specified).”

**157.** `[C0043 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Rules of order—whether contained in the _____ authority or adopted as special rules of order—can be suspended by a two-thirds vote as explained in 25 (with the exceptions there specified).
   A. parliamentary
   B. constitution
   C. resolutions
   D. consideration

   *Answer:* A) parliamentary. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:21 — “Rules of order—whether contained in the parliamentary authority or adopted as special rules of order—can be suspended by a two-thirds vote as explained in 25 (with the exceptions there specified).”

**158.** `[C0043 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Rules clearly _____ as in the nature of rules of order that are placed within the bylaws can (with the exceptions specified in 25) also be suspended by a two-thirds vote;

   *Answer:* identifiable — “Rules clearly identifiable as in the nature of rules of order that are placed within the bylaws can (with the exceptions specified in 25) also be suspended by a two-thirds vote;”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:21 — “Rules clearly identifiable as in the nature of rules of order that are placed within the bylaws can (with the exceptions specified in 25) also be suspended by a two-thirds vote;”

**159.** `[C0043 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: but, except for such rules and for clauses that provide for their own _____, as stated above, rules in the bylaws cannot be suspended.
   A. suspension
   B. sustaining
   C. authorizes
   D. committees

   *Answer:* A) suspension. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:21 — “but, except for such rules and for clauses that provide for their own suspension, as stated above, rules in the bylaws cannot be suspended.”

**160.** `[C0043 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Rules clearly _____ as in the nature of rules of order that are placed within the bylaws can (with the exceptions specified in 25) also be suspended by a two-thirds vote;
   A. adjournment
   B. identifiable
   C. Convention
   D. Indefinitely

   *Answer:* B) identifiable. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:21 — “Rules clearly identifiable as in the nature of rules of order that are placed within the bylaws can (with the exceptions specified in 25) also be suspended by a two-thirds vote;”

**161.** `[C0044 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: After the bylaws of a society have been initially adopted when the _____ is formed, the adoption or amendment of special rules of order placed within the bylaws is subject to the procedure for amending the bylaws (see 57).

   *Answer:* organization — “After the bylaws of a society have been initially adopted when the organization is formed, the adoption or amendment of special rules of order placed within the bylaws is subject to the procedure for amending the bylaws (see 57).”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:22 — “After the bylaws of a society have been initially adopted when the organization is formed, the adoption or amendment of special rules of order placed within the bylaws is subject to the procedure for amending the bylaws (see 57).”

**162.** `[C0044 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: After the bylaws of a society have been initially adopted when the _____ is formed, the adoption or amendment of special rules of order placed within the bylaws is subject to the procedure for amending the bylaws (see 57).
   A. organization
   B. immediately
   C. Preparation
   D. constitution

   *Answer:* A) organization. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:22 — “After the bylaws of a society have been initially adopted when the organization is formed, the adoption or amendment of special rules of order placed within the bylaws is subject to the procedure for amending the bylaws (see 57).”

**163.** `[C0045 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: _____, as understood in this book except in the case of conventions, are rules (1) which are related to the details of the administration of a society rather than to parliamentary procedure, and (2) which can be adopted or changed upon the same conditions as any ordinary act of the society.

   *Answer:* Standing rules — “Standing rules, as understood in this book except in the case of conventions, are rules (1) which are related to the details of the administration of a society rather than to parliamentary procedure, and (2) which can be adopted or changed upon the same conditions as any ordinary act of the society.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:23 — “Standing rules, as understood in this book except in the case of conventions, are rules (1) which are related to the details of the administration of a society rather than to parliamentary procedure, and (2) which can be adopted or changed upon the same conditions as any ordinary act of the society.”

**164.** `[C0045 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: As with special rules of order, it is advisable for standing rules to be printed under a separate heading in the booklet containing the bylaws, and in such a case, any enacting words such as “_____, That” are normally dropped.
   A. meetings
   B. division
   C. Resolved
   D. Approved

   *Answer:* C) Resolved. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:23 — “As with special rules of order, it is advisable for standing rules to be printed under a separate heading in the booklet containing the bylaws, and in such a case, any enacting words such as “Resolved, That” are normally dropped.”

**165.** `[C0045 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: A standing rule can be adopted by a _____ vote without previous notice, provided that it does not conflict with or amend any existing rule or act of the society.

   *Answer:* majority — “A standing rule can be adopted by a majority vote without previous notice, provided that it does not conflict with or amend any existing rule or act of the society.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:23 — “A standing rule can be adopted by a majority vote without previous notice, provided that it does not conflict with or amend any existing rule or act of the society.”

**166.** `[C0045 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Standing rules, as understood in this book except in the case of conventions, are rules (1) which are related to the details of the _____ of a society rather than to parliamentary procedure, and (2) which can be adopted or changed upon the same conditions as any ordinary act of the society.
   A. Parliamentarian
   B. deliberative
   C. administration
   D. Announcements

   *Answer:* C) administration. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:23 — “Standing rules, as understood in this book except in the case of conventions, are rules (1) which are related to the details of the administration of a society rather than to parliamentary procedure, and (2) which can be adopted or changed upon the same conditions as any ordinary act of the society.”

**167.** `[C0045 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: An example of such a rule might be one setting the hour at which meetings are to begin, or one relating to the _____ of a guest register.

   *Answer:* maintenance — “An example of such a rule might be one setting the hour at which meetings are to begin, or one relating to the maintenance of a guest register.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:23 — “An example of such a rule might be one setting the hour at which meetings are to begin, or one relating to the maintenance of a guest register.”

**168.** `[C0046 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Some assemblies, particularly legislative bodies, also apply the name _____ to their rules of order.

   *Answer:* standing rules — “Some assemblies, particularly legislative bodies, also apply the name standing rules to their rules of order.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:24 — “Some assemblies, particularly legislative bodies, also apply the name standing rules to their rules of order.”

**169.** `[C0046 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Some assemblies, _____ legislative bodies, also apply the name standing rules to their rules of order.
   A. adjournment
   B. resolutions
   C. particularly
   D. Reconsider

   *Answer:* C) particularly. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:24 — “Some assemblies, particularly legislative bodies, also apply the name standing rules to their rules of order.”

**170.** `[C0046 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Whatever names an assembly may apply to its various rules, the vote required to adopt, amend, or suspend a particular rule is determined by the nature of its content according to the _____ given above.

   *Answer:* definitions — “Whatever names an assembly may apply to its various rules, the vote required to adopt, amend, or suspend a particular rule is determined by the nature of its content according to the definitions given above.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:24 — “Whatever names an assembly may apply to its various rules, the vote required to adopt, amend, or suspend a particular rule is determined by the nature of its content according to the definitions given above.”

**171.** `[C0046 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Whatever names an assembly may apply to its various rules, the vote required to adopt, amend, or suspend a particular rule is determined by the nature of its content according to the _____ given above.
   A. Permission
   B. Organization
   C. committees
   D. definitions

   *Answer:* D) definitions. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:24 — “Whatever names an assembly may apply to its various rules, the vote required to adopt, amend, or suspend a particular rule is determined by the nature of its content according to the definitions given above.”

**172.** `[C0047 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: However, if a customary practice is or becomes in conflict with the parliamentary authority or any written rule, and a _____ (23) citing the conflict is raised at any time, the custom falls to the ground, and the conflicting provision in the parliamentary authority or written rule must thereafter be complied with.

   *Answer:* Point of Order — “However, if a customary practice is or becomes in conflict with the parliamentary authority or any written rule, and a Point of Order (23) citing the conflict is raised at any time, the custom falls to the ground, and the conflicting provision in the parliamentary authority or written rule must thereafter be complied with.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:25 — “However, if a customary practice is or becomes in conflict with the parliamentary authority or any written rule, and a Point of Order (23) citing the conflict is raised at any time, the custom falls to the ground, and the conflicting provision in the parliamentary authority or written rule must thereafter be complied with.”

**173.** `[C0047 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: If there is no contrary provision in the parliamentary authority or written rules of the organization, such an established custom is adhered to unless the assembly, by a _____ vote, agrees in a particular instance to do otherwise.
   A. A unanimous (general consent) vote
   B. A three-fourths vote
   C. A majority vote
   D. A two-thirds vote

   *Answer:* C) A majority vote. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:25 — “If there is no contrary provision in the parliamentary authority or written rules of the organization, such an established custom is adhered to unless the assembly, by a majority vote, agrees in a particular instance to do otherwise.”

**174.** `[C0047 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: In some _____, a particular practice may sometimes come to be followed as a matter of established custom so that it is treated practically as if it were prescribed by a rule.

   *Answer:* organizations — “In some organizations, a particular practice may sometimes come to be followed as a matter of established custom so that it is treated practically as if it were prescribed by a rule.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:25 — “In some organizations, a particular practice may sometimes come to be followed as a matter of established custom so that it is treated practically as if it were prescribed by a rule.”

**175.** `[C0047 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: If there is no contrary provision in the _____ authority or written rules of the organization, such an established custom is adhered to unless the assembly, by a majority vote, agrees in a particular instance to do otherwise.
   A. requirement
   B. Indefinitely
   C. parliamentary
   D. adjournment

   *Answer:* C) parliamentary. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:25 — “If there is no contrary provision in the parliamentary authority or written rules of the organization, such an established custom is adhered to unless the assembly, by a majority vote, agrees in a particular instance to do otherwise.”

**176.** `[C0047 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: If it is then desired to follow the former practice, a special rule of order (or, in appropriate _____, a standing rule or a bylaw provision) can be added or amended to incorporate it.

   *Answer:* circumstances — “If it is then desired to follow the former practice, a special rule of order (or, in appropriate circumstances, a standing rule or a bylaw provision) can be added or amended to incorporate it.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:25 — “If it is then desired to follow the former practice, a special rule of order (or, in appropriate circumstances, a standing rule or a bylaw provision) can be added or amended to incorporate it.”

**177.** `[C0048 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The basic parliamentary concepts and practices are _____ in such a way that a complete statement of the rules that relate to any one of them frequently involves reference to several other concepts.

   *Answer:* interconnected — “The basic parliamentary concepts and practices are interconnected in such a way that a complete statement of the rules that relate to any one of them frequently involves reference to several other concepts.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:1 — “The basic parliamentary concepts and practices are interconnected in such a way that a complete statement of the rules that relate to any one of them frequently involves reference to several other concepts.”

**178.** `[C0048 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: This section contains an initial _____ of a number of these topics, which are given a more detailed treatment later in this book.
   A. Indefinitely
   B. explanation
   C. resolutions
   D. Incidental

   *Answer:* B) explanation. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:1 — “This section contains an initial explanation of a number of these topics, which are given a more detailed treatment later in this book.”

**179.** `[C0048 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The basic parliamentary concepts and practices are _____ in such a way that a complete statement of the rules that relate to any one of them frequently involves reference to several other concepts.
   A. interconnected
   B. consideration
   C. presentation
   D. characteristics

   *Answer:* A) interconnected. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:1 — “The basic parliamentary concepts and practices are interconnected in such a way that a complete statement of the rules that relate to any one of them frequently involves reference to several other concepts.”

**180.** `[C0048 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: This section contains an initial _____ of a number of these topics, which are given a more detailed treatment later in this book.

   *Answer:* explanation — “This section contains an initial explanation of a number of these topics, which are given a more detailed treatment later in this book.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:1 — “This section contains an initial explanation of a number of these topics, which are given a more detailed treatment later in this book.”

**181.** `[C0049 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: In reading all that follows _____ this manual, it should be borne in mind that—as in any treatment of any subject—a statement of a rule generally cannot include all possible exceptions to the rule.

   *Answer:* throughout — “In reading all that follows throughout this manual, it should be borne in mind that—as in any treatment of any subject—a statement of a rule generally cannot include all possible exceptions to the rule.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:2 — “In reading all that follows throughout this manual, it should be borne in mind that—as in any treatment of any subject—a statement of a rule generally cannot include all possible exceptions to the rule.”

**182.** `[C0049 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: In reading all that follows _____ this manual, it should be borne in mind that—as in any treatment of any subject—a statement of a rule generally cannot include all possible exceptions to the rule.
   A. authorizes
   B. substitute
   C. persuasive
   D. throughout

   *Answer:* D) throughout. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:2 — “In reading all that follows throughout this manual, it should be borne in mind that—as in any treatment of any subject—a statement of a rule generally cannot include all possible exceptions to the rule.”

**183.** `[C0050 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The minimum number of members who must be present at the meetings of a deliberative assembly for business to be validly transacted is the _____ of the assembly.
   A. quorum
   B. Appeal
   C. accept
   D. ballot

   *Answer:* A) quorum. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:3 — “The minimum number of members who must be present at the meetings of a deliberative assembly for business to be validly transacted is the quorum of the assembly.”

**184.** `[C0050 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: In both houses of Congress, the quorum is a _____ of the members, by the United States Constitution.

   *Answer:* majority — “In both houses of Congress, the quorum is a majority of the members, by the United States Constitution.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:3 — “In both houses of Congress, the quorum is a majority of the members, by the United States Constitution.”

**185.** `[C0050 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The minimum number of members who must be present at the meetings of a _____ assembly for business to be validly transacted is the quorum of the assembly.
   A. Particular
   B. deliberative
   C. Organization
   D. specification

   *Answer:* B) deliberative. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:3 — “The minimum number of members who must be present at the meetings of a deliberative assembly for business to be validly transacted is the quorum of the assembly.”

**186.** `[C0050 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The requirement of a quorum is a protection against totally _____ action in the name of the body by an unduly small number of persons.

   *Answer:* unrepresentative — “The requirement of a quorum is a protection against totally unrepresentative action in the name of the body by an unduly small number of persons.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:3 — “The requirement of a quorum is a protection against totally unrepresentative action in the name of the body by an unduly small number of persons.”

**187.** `[C0050 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: In both houses of Congress, the quorum is a majority of the members, by the United States _____.
   A. Reconsider
   B. Organization
   C. Constitution
   D. Indefinitely

   *Answer:* C) Constitution. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:3 — “In both houses of Congress, the quorum is a majority of the members, by the United States Constitution.”

**188.** `[C0051 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: In the meetings of a convention, unless the bylaws of the organization provide otherwise, the quorum is a _____ of the delegates who have been registered at the convention as in attendance, irrespective of whether some may have departed.

   *Answer:* majority — “In the meetings of a convention, unless the bylaws of the organization provide otherwise, the quorum is a majority of the delegates who have been registered at the convention as in attendance, irrespective of whether some may have departed.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:4 — “In the meetings of a convention, unless the bylaws of the organization provide otherwise, the quorum is a majority of the delegates who have been registered at the convention as in attendance, irrespective of whether some may have departed.”

**189.** `[C0051 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: In the meetings of a convention, unless the bylaws of the _____ provide otherwise, the quorum is a majority of the delegates who have been registered at the convention as in attendance, irrespective of whether some may have departed.
   A. requirement
   B. interrupted
   C. resolutions
   D. organization

   *Answer:* D) organization. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:4 — “In the meetings of a convention, unless the bylaws of the organization provide otherwise, the quorum is a majority of the delegates who have been registered at the convention as in attendance, irrespective of whether some may have departed.”

**190.** `[C0051 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: In a mass meeting, or in a regular or properly called meeting of an organization whose bylaws do not prescribe a quorum and whose membership is loosely determined (as, for example, in many church _____ or alumni associations), there is no minimum number of members who must be present for the valid transaction of business, or—as it is usually expressed— the quorum consists of those who attend the meeting.

   *Answer:* congregations — “In a mass meeting, or in a regular or properly called meeting of an organization whose bylaws do not prescribe a quorum and whose membership is loosely determined (as, for example, in many church congregations or alumni associations), there is no minimum number of members who must be present for the valid transaction of business, or—as it is usually expressed— the quorum consists of those who attend the meeting.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:4 — “In a mass meeting, or in a regular or properly called meeting of an organization whose bylaws do not prescribe a quorum and whose membership is loosely determined (as, for example, in many church congregations or alumni associations), there is no minimum number of members who must be present for the valid transaction of business, or—as it is usually expressed— the quorum consists of those who attend the meeting.”

**191.** `[C0051 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: In a mass meeting, or in a regular or properly called meeting of an organization whose bylaws do not prescribe a quorum and whose membership is loosely determined (as, for example, in many church _____ or alumni associations), there is no minimum number of members who must be present for the valid transaction of business, or—as it is usually expressed— the quorum consists of those who attend the meeting.
   A. representatives
   B. congregations
   C. Parliamentarian
   D. Parliamentary

   *Answer:* B) congregations. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:4 — “In a mass meeting, or in a regular or properly called meeting of an organization whose bylaws do not prescribe a quorum and whose membership is loosely determined (as, for example, in many church congregations or alumni associations), there is no minimum number of members who must be present for the valid transaction of business, or—as it is usually expressed— the quorum consists of those who attend the meeting.”

**192.** `[C0052 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The minimum essential officers for the conduct of business in a deliberative assembly are a _____ officer, who conducts the meeting and sees that the rules are observed, and a secretary, or clerk, who makes a written record of what is done—usually called “the minutes.” If the officers are members of the assembly—as they usually are in ordinary societies—they are counted in determining whether a quorum is present.
   A. presiding
   B. expressly
   C. secondary
   D. committee

   *Answer:* A) presiding. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:6 — “The minimum essential officers for the conduct of business in a deliberative assembly are a presiding officer, who conducts the meeting and sees that the rules are observed, and a secretary, or clerk, who makes a written record of what is done—usually called “the minutes.” If the officers are members of the assembly—as they usually are in ordinary societies—they are counted in determining whether a quorum is present.”

**193.** `[C0052 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The minimum essential _____s for the conduct of business in a deliberative assembly are a presiding officer, who conducts the meeting and sees that the rules are observed, and a secretary, or clerk, who makes a written record of what is done—usually called “the minutes.” If the officers are members of the assembly—as they usually are in ordinary societies—they are counted in determining whether a quorum is present.

   *Answer:* officer — “The minimum essential officers for the conduct of business in a deliberative assembly are a presiding officer, who conducts the meeting and sees that the rules are observed, and a secretary, or clerk, who makes a written record of what is done—usually called “the minutes.” If the officers are members of the assembly—as they usually are in ordinary societies—they are counted in determining whether a quorum is present.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:6 — “The minimum essential officers for the conduct of business in a deliberative assembly are a presiding officer, who conducts the meeting and sees that the rules are observed, and a secretary, or clerk, who makes a written record of what is done—usually called “the minutes.” If the officers are members of the assembly—as they usually are in ordinary societies—they are counted in determining whether a quorum is present.”

**194.** `[C0053 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The _____ officer’s official place or station (usually in the center of the platform or stage, if there is one) is called “the chair.” During meetings, whoever is presiding is said to be “in the chair” (whether standing or seated at the time), and he is also referred to as “the chair.” The phrase “the chair” thus applies both to the person presiding and to his station in the hall from which he presides.

   *Answer:* presiding — “The presiding officer’s official place or station (usually in the center of the platform or stage, if there is one) is called “the chair.” During meetings, whoever is presiding is said to be “in the chair” (whether standing or seated at the time), and he is also referred to as “the chair.” The phrase “the chair” thus applies both to the person presiding and to his station in the hall from which he presides.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:7 — “The presiding officer’s official place or station (usually in the center of the platform or stage, if there is one) is called “the chair.” During meetings, whoever is presiding is said to be “in the chair” (whether standing or seated at the time), and he is also referred to as “the chair.” The phrase “the chair” thus applies both to the person presiding and to his station in the hall from which he presides.”

**195.** `[C0053 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The _____’s desk should be placed so that papers can easily be passed to him from the chair during the meeting.
   A. secretary
   B. Crestwood
   C. paragraph
   D. committee

   *Answer:* A) secretary. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:7 — “The secretary’s desk should be placed so that papers can easily be passed to him from the chair during the meeting.”

**196.** `[C0053 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The _____ officer’s official place or station (usually in the center of the platform or stage, if there is one) is called “the chair.” During meetings, whoever is presiding is said to be “in the chair” (whether standing or seated at the time), and he is also referred to as “the chair.” The phrase “the chair” thus applies both to the person presiding and to his station in the hall from which he presides.
   A. Privilege
   B. secretary
   C. presiding
   D. exhausted

   *Answer:* C) presiding. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:7 — “The presiding officer’s official place or station (usually in the center of the platform or stage, if there is one) is called “the chair.” During meetings, whoever is presiding is said to be “in the chair” (whether standing or seated at the time), and he is also referred to as “the chair.” The phrase “the chair” thus applies both to the person presiding and to his station in the hall from which he presides.”

**197.** `[C0053 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The _____’s desk should be placed so that papers can easily be passed to him from the chair during the meeting.

   *Answer:* secretary — “The secretary’s desk should be placed so that papers can easily be passed to him from the chair during the meeting.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:7 — “The secretary’s desk should be placed so that papers can easily be passed to him from the chair during the meeting.”

See RONR (12th ed.) §3:8 — “The duties of the presiding officer, the secretary, and other officers that an assembly or society may have are described in 47.”

**199.** `[C0054 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The duties of the _____ officer, the secretary, and other officers that an assembly or society may have are described in 47.
   A. rejecting
   B. exhausted
   C. presiding
   D. paragraph

   *Answer:* C) presiding. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:8 — “The duties of the presiding officer, the secretary, and other officers that an assembly or society may have are described in 47.”

**200.** `[C0055 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Customs of formality that are followed by the presiding officer and members under _____ procedure serve to maintain the chair’s necessary position of impartiality and help to preserve an objective and impersonal approach, especially when serious divisions of opinion arise.

   *Answer:* parliamentary — “Customs of formality that are followed by the presiding officer and members under parliamentary procedure serve to maintain the chair’s necessary position of impartiality and help to preserve an objective and impersonal approach, especially when serious divisions of opinion arise.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:9 — “Customs of formality that are followed by the presiding officer and members under parliamentary procedure serve to maintain the chair’s necessary position of impartiality and help to preserve an objective and impersonal approach, especially when serious divisions of opinion arise.”

**201.** `[C0055 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Customs of formality that are followed by the presiding officer and members under _____ procedure serve to maintain the chair’s necessary position of impartiality and help to preserve an objective and impersonal approach, especially when serious divisions of opinion arise.
   A. parliamentary
   B. recommendation
   C. Organization
   D. president-elect

   *Answer:* A) parliamentary. **202.** `[C0056 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The president or chief officer of an _____ society, who normally presides at its meetings, is then addressed as “Mr.
   A. execution
   B. Reporting
   C. Objection
   D. organized

   *Answer:* D) organized. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:10 — “The president or chief officer of an organized society, who normally presides at its meetings, is then addressed as “Mr.”

**203.** `[C0056 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: President” or “Madam President” (whether a married or _____ woman), “Mr.

   *Answer:* unmarried — “President” or “Madam President” (whether a married or unmarried woman), “Mr.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:10 — “President” or “Madam President” (whether a married or unmarried woman), “Mr.”

**204.** `[C0056 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: [or Madam] _____,” or by whatever may be his or her official title.
   A. reception
   B. Moderator
   C. Crestwood
   D. debatable

   *Answer:* B) Moderator. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:10 — “[or Madam] Moderator,” or by whatever may be his or her official title.”

**205.** `[C0056 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: In the lower house of a _____ body, this officer is most commonly “Mr.

   *Answer:* legislative — “In the lower house of a legislative body, this officer is most commonly “Mr.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:10 — “In the lower house of a legislative body, this officer is most commonly “Mr.”

**206.** `[C0056 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: [or Madam] Speaker.” A _____ is addressed as “Mr.
   A. specification
   B. Announcements
   C. vice-president
   D. Indefinitely

   *Answer:* C) vice-president. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:10 — “[or Madam] Speaker.” A vice-president is addressed as “Mr.”

**207.** `[C0057 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Even in a small meeting, the presiding officer of an _____ is not addressed or referred to by name.

   *Answer:* assembly — “Even in a small meeting, the presiding officer of an assembly is not addressed or referred to by name.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:11 — “Even in a small meeting, the presiding officer of an assembly is not addressed or referred to by name.”

**208.** `[C0057 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Even in a small meeting, the _____ officer of an assembly is not addressed or referred to by name.
   A. Secretary
   B. paragraph
   C. secondary
   D. presiding

   *Answer:* D) presiding. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:11 — “Even in a small meeting, the presiding officer of an assembly is not addressed or referred to by name.”

**209.** `[C0057 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: (The only exceptions that might arise in an assembly would be in cases of a testimonial nature, such as in the presentation of a gift to a president who is about to go out of office.) With nearly the same strictness of observance, he is not addressed by the personal pronoun “you”—although occasional exceptions may occur in ordinary societies if brief _____ consultation takes place during a meeting.

   *Answer:* administrative — “(The only exceptions that might arise in an assembly would be in cases of a testimonial nature, such as in the presentation of a gift to a president who is about to go out of office.) With nearly the same strictness of observance, he is not addressed by the personal pronoun “you”—although occasional exceptions may occur in ordinary societies if brief administrative consultation takes place during a meeting.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:11 — “(The only exceptions that might arise in an assembly would be in cases of a testimonial nature, such as in the presentation of a gift to a president who is about to go out of office.) With nearly the same strictness of observance, he is not addressed by the personal pronoun “you”—although occasional exceptions may occur in ordinary societies if brief administrative consultation takes place during a meeting.”

**210.** `[C0057 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: As a general rule, when _____ reference to the presiding officer is necessary in connection with addressing him by his official title, members speak of him as “the chair”—as in, “Mr.
   A. Procedures
   B. settlement
   C. additional
   D. Reconsider

   *Answer:* C) additional. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:11 — “As a general rule, when additional reference to the presiding officer is necessary in connection with addressing him by his official title, members speak of him as “the chair”—as in, “Mr.”

**211.** `[C0057 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: President, do I _____ the chair to state…?”

   *Answer:* understand — “President, do I understand the chair to state…?””. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:11 — “President, do I understand the chair to state…?””

**212.** `[C0058 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Members _____ only the chair, or address each other through the chair.
   A. Adjourn
   B. address
   C. carried
   D. Another

   *Answer:* B) address. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:12 — “Members address only the chair, or address each other through the chair.”

**213.** `[C0058 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: In the _____ transaction of business —within a latitude appropriate to the conditions of the particular body—members generally should try to avoid mentioning another member’s name whenever the person involved can be described in some other way, as in, “Mr.

   *Answer:* parliamentary — “In the parliamentary transaction of business —within a latitude appropriate to the conditions of the particular body—members generally should try to avoid mentioning another member’s name whenever the person involved can be described in some other way, as in, “Mr.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:12 — “In the parliamentary transaction of business —within a latitude appropriate to the conditions of the particular body—members generally should try to avoid mentioning another member’s name whenever the person involved can be described in some other way, as in, “Mr.”

**214.** `[C0058 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: President, may I ask the member to _____…,” or, “Mr.
   A. approve
   B. Program
   C. explain
   D. Inquiry

   *Answer:* C) explain. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:12 — “President, may I ask the member to explain…,” or, “Mr.”

**215.** `[C0058 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Chairman, I hope that the gentleman who last spoke will think of the probable _____…” With a very limited number of particular exceptions, and except in committees and small boards, a member never speaks while seated;

   *Answer:* consequences — “Chairman, I hope that the gentleman who last spoke will think of the probable consequences…” With a very limited number of particular exceptions, and except in committees and small boards, a member never speaks while seated;”. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:12 — “Chairman, I hope that the gentleman who last spoke will think of the probable consequences…” With a very limited number of particular exceptions, and except in committees and small boards, a member never speaks while seated;”

**216.** `[C0058 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Members _____ only the chair, or address each other through the chair.

   *Answer:* address — “Members address only the chair, or address each other through the chair.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:12 — “Members address only the chair, or address each other through the chair.”

**217.** `[C0059 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Customs Observed by the _____ Officer.
   A. Objection
   B. paragraph
   C. Precedent
   D. Presiding

   *Answer:* D) Presiding. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:13 — “Customs Observed by the Presiding Officer.”

**218.** `[C0059 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Customs Observed by the _____ Officer.

   *Answer:* Presiding — “Customs Observed by the Presiding Officer.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:13 — “Customs Observed by the Presiding Officer.”
